review_item: The paper claims that the neural network results for the homogeneous electron gas (HEG) "outperform many traditional ab initio methods" and achieve "error of less than 1%" using BF-DMC as reference. However, subsequent independent analysis in the literature demonstrates that the DeepSolid method (referred to as "LiNet") does not actually improve upon backflow DMC (BF-DMC) results across the full density regime for N=54 electrons, undermining the claimed superiority of the method for this benchmark system. "Overall, our neural network performs very well, with an error of less than 1% in a wide range of density (see Supplementary Table 11)." This claim presents the correlation error relative to BF-DMC, but the framing suggests the neural network method is achieving higher accuracy than traditional methods. A subsequent study by Pescia et al. (2024) in Physical Review B directly compared DeepSolid (labeled "LiNet") against BF-DMC for N=54 electrons and found that "This is in stark contrast to the (FermiNet-based) architecture of [27] (dubbed LiNet in the following), which does not improve upon BF-DMC energies over the whole density regime" [[1]](#citation-1). This directly contradicts the paper's implication of superior performance. "the obtained results, e.g. total energies, dissociation curves, and cohesive energies, outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches" The abstract makes a broad claim of outperforming "many traditional ab initio methods." However, for HEG specifically, the neural network VMC results remain at or slightly above BF-DMC energies due to the inherent variational bias. The paper's HEG Table 11 in the supplementary material shows energies that are systematically higher (less accurate) than BF-DMC at most densities, which is expected for a variational method but contradicts claims of "outperforming" state-of-the-art approaches. "The state-of-the-art results, namely VMC with backflow correlation (BF) [22] and distinguishable cluster with double excitations (DCD) [23] are also plotted for comparison, and the most accurate BF-DMC result is used as the reference energy of correlation error." By using BF-DMC as the reference and only comparing against BF-VMC and DCD, the paper obscures the fact that the neural network VMC cannot provide energies lower than BF-DMC due to the variational principle. The message-passing NQS study [[1]](#citation-1) shows that more recent neural network approaches can achieve lower energies than DeepSolid while using fewer parameters, suggesting the DeepSolid architecture is not optimal for this system. References: [1] Pescia, G., Nys, J., Kim, J., Lovato, A. & Carleo, G. Message-Passing Neural Quantum States for the Homogeneous Electron Gas. [Phys. Rev. B 110, 035108 (2024)](https://journals.aps.org/prb/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevB.110.035108); arXiv:2305.07240.

The manuscript’s headline accuracy claims for *cohesive energies* (graphene and LiH) are not fully supported because the key isolated-atom reference energies are imported from an external neural-QMC work rather than computed within the paper’s own method/code, which undermines attribution of the reported agreement to the proposed periodic ansatz and weakens reproducibility of the cohesive-energy results. “Graphene is arguably the most famous two-dimensional system (Fig. 2c) receiving broad attention in the past two decades for its mechanical, electronic, and chemical applications [24]. Here we carry out simulations to estimate its cohesive energy, which measures the strength of C-C chemical bonding and long-range dispersion interactions. The calculations are performed on a \( 2 \times 2 \) supercell of graphene using twist average boundary condition (TABC) [25] in conjunction with structure factor S(k) correction [26] (see Supplementary Fig. 3) to reduce the finite-size error. The calculated results are plotted in Fig. 2d along with the experimental value [21], and it shows that our neural network can deal with graphene very well, producing a cohesive energy of graphene within 0.1 eV/atom to the experimental reference (see Supplementary Table 6). We also plotted the results with PBC, namely the Γ point only result, which deviates from the experiment data by 1.25 eV/atom.” This is a strong quantitative conclusion (“within 0.1 eV/atom”) that depends on how the cohesive energy is constructed, not just on the bulk/supercell total energy. Cohesive energies are differences between large total energies, so the atomic reference must be consistent and clearly attributable to the proposed method to justify the claim. Here, the paper presents the cohesive-energy agreement as evidence that “our neural network can deal with graphene very well,” which implicitly attributes the result to the proposed periodic ansatz. If the atomic reference energy is taken from a different method/pipeline, the claim no longer isolates the contribution of the proposed approach. The reader cannot tell whether the agreement is driven primarily by the graphene calculation, the external atomic reference, or cancellation between them. This weakens the inferential link from the graphene cohesive-energy number to the paper’s core methodological contribution. “The final results are listed in Supplementary Table 6. The energy of an isolated carbon atom is taken from Ref. [2], \( E = -37.84471 \) Hartree.” This sentence explicitly confirms that a crucial input to the graphene cohesive energy (the isolated-atom energy) is not produced by the present work’s workflow, but copied from another paper. While using an external benchmark value can be reasonable for contextual comparison, it materially changes what is being validated: the reported cohesive energy is no longer an end-to-end result of the proposed periodic neural network ansatz. Because the paper’s conclusion is phrased as the method “producing” an accurate cohesive energy, the computational provenance of all terms in the energy difference matters. In particular, any mismatch in Hamiltonian conventions, numerical settings, or physical corrections between the external atom calculation and the present periodic calculation would directly propagate into the cohesive energy. The manuscript does not provide a justification that such mismatches are negligible at the claimed 0.1 eV/atom level. “The energy of \(2 \times 2 \times 2\) LiH is listed in Supplementary Table 8. The energy of an isolated lithium atom is taken from Ref. [2], \(E = -7.47798\) Hartree.” The same pattern occurs for LiH: a cohesive-energy-related reference (the isolated Li atom) is imported from an external work rather than computed with the proposed approach. This makes it difficult to assess the method’s capability on an equal footing, because the cohesive energy is sensitive to the accuracy of *both* the bulk and atomic calculations. Importing atomic energies also impedes reproducibility: a reader re-running the provided DeepSolid configurations would not reproduce the reported cohesive energies without separately sourcing the same atomic numbers from elsewhere. More importantly, the manuscript uses the LiH cohesive-energy agreement as a validation highlight (“very close to the experimental value”), but that agreement is partly contingent on a value computed outside the presented methodology. The paper should either compute these atomic references with the same code path or clearly reframe the claim as “bulk energies + external atomic benchmarks,” not as an end-to-end cohesive-energy prediction. “C -37.84471(5)” [[1]](#ref1) This entry from FermiNet’s Table I matches the carbon atomic energy reused by the reviewed manuscript for its graphene cohesive-energy calculation. It demonstrates that a key term in the reported cohesive energy comes from an external neural-network VMC workflow rather than from the reviewed paper’s periodic-solid method. That matters because it changes what is being validated: the cohesive-energy agreement partially inherits accuracy from a separate implementation with its own training, sampling, and numerical choices. Even if the external atomic energy is highly accurate, the resulting cohesive energy is not an end-to-end prediction attributable solely to the proposed periodic ansatz. In addition, any mismatch in physical conventions or numerical corrections between the two workflows would directly enter the energy difference. Therefore, the cohesive-energy benchmark provides weaker evidence for the proposed method than implied by the manuscript’s interpretation. “The determination of the cohesive energy is obtained as being the difference between the energy of the isolated nickel and that of the bul.k per unit atom.” [[4]](#ref4) This sentence states the standard definition of cohesive energy as a difference between an isolated-atom energy and a bulk per-atom energy, highlighting that the atomic reference is an essential part of the reported quantity. Because cohesive energies are differences between large numbers, careful control of consistency between the atomic and bulk computations is needed to avoid spurious agreement. In the reviewed manuscript, the isolated-atom terms are not computed within the same reported workflow, so the cohesive energies are not fully “closed” results of the proposed method. The citation also underscores why importing atomic energies is not a minor bookkeeping choice: it can dominate the error budget for the derived quantity. Therefore, presenting cohesive-energy agreement as a primary accuracy demonstration requires more methodological closure than the paper currently provides. This gap materially weakens the strength of the paper’s validation evidence. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf) [2] C. Lin, F. H. Zong, and D. M. Ceperley. *Twist-averaged boundary conditions in continuum quantum Monte Carlo algorithms* (Phys. Rev. E 64, 016702). [https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf](https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf) [4] E. H. Megchiche, C. Mijoule, M. Amarouche, and J.-C. Barthelat. *First-principles calculations of the vacancy formation energy in nickel* (Algerian Journal of Advanced Materials, 2006). [https://hal.science/hal-04289125/document](https://hal.science/hal-04289125/document)

The paper compares the properties of Lithium Hydride (LiH) derived from a small $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell directly to experimental bulk values, ignoring significant finite-size errors. "where $E_0, V_0, B_0, B'_0$ are fitted quantities, their results and corresponding experiment data [4] are listed in Supplementary Table 9." The authors fit the equation of state and compare the resulting bulk modulus ($B_0$) and cohesive energy ($E_{coh}$) directly to experimental values. However, the data used for this fit comes from the $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell calculations (Supplementary Table 8), as indicated by the context of Supplementary Note 4.3 and 4.4. "Energy of the $3 \times 3 \times 3$ LiH crystal... -8.16020(2)" (Supplementary Table 10) The energy per primitive cell for the $3 \times 3 \times 3$ supercell at $L=4.061$ Å is -8.16020 Ha, whereas the $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell energy at $L=4.0$ Å is -8.15096 Ha (Supplementary Table 8). This difference of approximately 10 mHa indicates a substantial finite-size error that has not been converged or extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit. "Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions" [3] As established in the literature, finite-size errors in many-body calculations of solids, especially for potential energy, decay slowly ($1/L$ or $1/N$). Comparing results from a small supercell directly to bulk experiment without proper finite-size scaling or extrapolation leads to invalid conclusions about the method's accuracy relative to experiment. References: [3] S. Chiesa et al., "Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions", Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404 (2006). https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404 [4] P. López Ríos et al., "Inhomogeneous backflow transformations in quantum Monte Carlo calculations", Phys. Rev. E 74, 066701 (2006). https://journals.aps.org/pre/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevE.74.066701

Applying neural network for ab initio calculations of real solids has been already proposed by another group (https://doi.org/10.1038/s42005-021-00609-0). The research group has extended the restricted Boltzmann Machine ansatz to real periodic systems, although it employs the second quantization and finite basis approximation. The authors should mention this work in the introduction.
The definition of 'Correlation error' in the Fig. 2 h is not given.
Supplementary Table 4 looks listing energies per atom. Please state this clearly.

The manuscript's claim that the method outperforms other ab initio methods is not supported by the evidence shown. For the H10 and the UEG, the result is better stated as similar performance with the state-of-the-art DMC results (rather than a claim of being better than common approaches). In the case of the UEG in particular, it is known that the DMC results are not free from error at high densities due to fixed node error. Instead, as highlighted by a recent paper (Ref 31), the most accurate approaches come from FCIQMC which are available for N=14. Similar FCIQMC results are also available for N=54, which could have been used for comparison as they show clear improvement on BF-DMC.

The paper repeatedly claims "near-exact potential energy surfaces" but employs minimal (STO-6G) and small (6-31G) basis sets for the key demonstrations, which are known to introduce substantial basis set incompleteness errors that can significantly affect both quantitative accuracy and qualitative behavior. "With a small number of accurate correlated wave functions as a training set, we demonstrate provable convergence to near-exact potential energy surfaces for subsequent dynamics" The claim of "near-exact" surfaces is misleading in the context of the basis sets used. The method converges to the exact solution within the given finite basis set (FCI limit), but this is not the same as being near the true physical limit. For the 30-atom hydrogen chain (Fig. 4), STO-6G basis is used, and for the Zundel cation (Fig. 5), 6-31G basis is used. Both are known to introduce significant basis set incompleteness errors. "Molecular dynamics of a 30 atom hydrogen chain in a STO-6G basis from an initially symmetrically stretched geometry." STO-6G is a minimal basis set with only one contracted Gaussian per orbital. While the eigenvector continuation may achieve exact energies within this basis, the basis set itself fundamentally cannot describe important physics such as polarization, dispersion interactions, or accurate bond dissociation energetics. The "qualitative differences" observed compared to DFT and HF may partly reflect basis set limitations rather than purely the treatment of electron correlation. "The complete basis set limit of −199.16704295 a.u. was obtained using a mixed Gaussian extrapolation scheme tailored for correlation-consistent basis sets" from [Numerically exact configuration interaction at quadrillion, Nature Communications 2025][2] This reference demonstrates that modern high-accuracy quantum chemistry routinely employs basis set extrapolation to the complete basis set (CBS) limit. The paper's use of minimal basis sets without any discussion of basis set convergence or extrapolation undermines the claim of "near-exact" surfaces, as the true physical answer may differ substantially from the FCI limit within these small basis sets. References: [2] [Larsson, H.R., Zhai, H., Umrigar, C.J., and Chan, G.K.-L. "The chromium dimer: Closing a chapter of quantum chemistry." J. Am. Chem. Soc. 144, 15932 (2022); see also basis set extrapolation in Nature Communications 16 (2025)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-65967-7)

The paper’s on-the-fly data selection (“active learning”) and trajectory-convergence stopping rule are not sufficient to establish that the inferred potential energy surface and forces are accurate, because the selection metric is heuristic and the stopping criterion measures only variational lowering between successive surrogate models rather than an error bound to the exact PES. “Since the (non-degenerate) ground states along the trajectory are uniquely defined by the *ab initio* Hamiltonian at each geometry, we use the differences between the Hamiltonian elements at the training points and all trajectory points in defining this measure.” This sentence shows that the selection strategy rests on a non-degeneracy assumption and uses Hamiltonian-element differences as a proxy for wave function similarity. In realistic chemical dynamics, near-degeneracies and avoided crossings are common, and even without exact degeneracy, rapid eigenvector changes can occur near branch points, which can break the proxy’s reliability. Even under non-degeneracy, “uniquely defined” does not imply that a simple least-squares distance between integrals is a calibrated indicator of eigenvector continuation error. As a result, the method can select points that change integrals substantially while missing points where the wave function changes sharply but integrals do not. The paper does not provide evidence (e.g., correlation plots between this distance and actual energy/force error) showing that the metric targets worst-case EC error. Therefore, the methodology does not reliably guarantee accuracy of the resulting PES. “Specifically, we define this Hamiltonian distance between two geometries, \( D(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{R}') \), as \[ D(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{R}') = \sum_{ij} |h^{(1)}_{ij}(\mathbf{R}) - h^{(1)}_{ij}(\mathbf{R}')|^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{ijkl} |h^{(2)}_{ijkl}(\mathbf{R}) - h^{(2)}_{ijkl}(\mathbf{R}')|^2. \]” This definition aggregates squared differences over all one- and two-electron integral elements, producing a large unnormalized scalar whose magnitude will strongly depend on basis size and indexing conventions. While the authors motivate the metric as symmetry-respecting in the SAO basis, it remains a heuristic surrogate for the actual EC projection error in energy and (critically for MD) forces. In particular, large sums over many integral elements can obscure which physically relevant operator changes matter most for the ground-state eigenvector. Moreover, the metric does not incorporate any direct information about the current reduced subspace’s ability to represent the target eigenstate (e.g., residual norms or subspace angles), which is standard in reduced-order modeling. The paper also does not report ablations comparing alternative selection criteria or showing that this choice yields consistent convergence rates across systems. Consequently, the data-selection method is not convincingly justified as a reliable error-control mechanism. “In our applications, we terminate the simulation when the predicted energy with the enlarged data set stays within a tolerance of \( \epsilon = 10^{-3}\ E_h \) along the full MD trajectory for two iterations in a row.” This stopping rule only ensures that adding another batch of training points (chosen by the same heuristic) no longer decreases the *variational upper bound* by more than a threshold along the *already-computed* trajectory. It does not bound the absolute error to the exact ground-state PES, because the true energy could still be above by more than \(10^{-3} E_h\) while successive subspace enlargements yield small further decreases. It also does not certify force accuracy, which is the quantity directly controlling dynamical fidelity and energy conservation in NVE MD. Additionally, because the trajectory itself changes when the PES changes (“each time the data set is enlarged, the PES changes and the MD follows a different path”), convergence measured on one trajectory does not directly translate to robustness under perturbations. In reduced-basis practice, termination criteria are commonly tied to residual-based estimators or certified error bounds, rather than only successive-model differences. Therefore, the paper’s convergence claims for MD are not rigorously supported by the reported stopping criterion. “Greedy algorithms require fast approximations of the emulator’s error and terminate when either a requested error tolerance or a maximum number of iterations has been achieved.” [[4]](#ref4) This describes a common reduced-basis practice: snapshot selection is guided by an explicit (even if approximate) error indicator and a termination condition tied to that indicator. In contrast, the reviewed manuscript uses an integral-distance heuristic without demonstrating that it approximates the EC energy/force error, and stops based on surrogate-to-surrogate variational lowering. The mismatch matters because the paper’s strongest claims depend on systematic convergence of the *true* PES relevant to MD, not just internal stabilization of the surrogate. Without an error estimator, it is difficult to ensure that the remaining error is below the stated threshold across the explored region of configuration space. The authors’ own admission that adding poorly converged training points can “negatively affect the performance of the data selection” further shows the fragility of a heuristic criterion. Taken together, the paper’s active-learning and convergence procedures fall short of common field standards for trustworthy error control. “the eigenvalue continuation approach has some fundamental limitations … it is not a self-converging method, i.e., it gives no tool to affirm that the results obtained are inaccurate or simply wrong.” [[5]](#ref5) This external critique targets the precise gap in the reviewed manuscript’s methodology: the lack of a self-contained diagnostic that certifies accuracy as training is added. The paper’s use of variational monotonicity and Hamiltonian-distance heuristics does not provide a guaranteed link to absolute accuracy, particularly in regions where the eigenvector changes rapidly or where training states are imperfect. Because the approach is used for MD, undetected force errors can accumulate into qualitatively different trajectories, making the need for a reliable error indicator more acute. The manuscript acknowledges that inaccurate training points can harm convergence of the selection scheme, which aligns with the concern that the procedure can fail silently. As a result, the methodology does not meet the bar needed to support “provable convergence” and robust MD conclusions. A validated estimator (or systematic reference checks on representative segments) would be needed to close this gap. References: [4] [C. Drischler et al., “BUQEYE Guide to Projection-Based Emulators in Nuclear Physics,” arXiv:2212.04912 (2022)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2212.04912.pdf) [5] [Tomasz Sowiński and Miguel A. Garcia-March, “Fundamental limitations of the eigenvalue continuation approach,” arXiv:2202.07493 (2022)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2202.07493.pdf)

The paper characterizes the approach as "practical" and "black-box", but this overlooks the significant complexity and fragility of generating the required high-accuracy training data. "inference of properties from the model ... rendering this a practical scheme." While the inference step is robust, the overall workflow depends entirely on the generation of accurate training wave functions (e.g., using DMRG or CAS). The paper itself admits that this step is prone to failure. "At a number of these training geometries, the HF and/or CCSD were not able to fully converge... DMRG training data was not able to correctly converge" The authors acknowledge that generating training data is difficult and failed for a significant fraction of points (e.g., 13 points for Zundel cation). This contradicts the earlier claim of the method being a "practical" solution that overcomes the lack of black-box character in correlated methods, as the method's success is contingent on the very calculations that are described as non-black-box.

In the idea of moving to more complex and relevant chemical systems, made of more particles, it is not clear which limitations would be faced in the applications of the approach. Is it how infeasible would be to calculate several training points with accurate wavefunctions? Is it the number of training points to reach convergence that would drastically increase? Is the complexity of the potential energy surface that would not allow it?

In Fig. 6, the instability of CAS-based dynamics is very pronounced. While the DMRG curves look sensible, CAS-based eigenvector continuation fails due to the inclusion of very disparate CAS spaces along the trajectory. When no crossing of BO surfaces is present, CASSCF with sensible choice (such as full valence space) should fix it. Alternatively, using automatic generation of valence active space (AVAS) might circumvent the issues without CASSCF. If crossing across different BO surfaces, more complexity is involved to interpolate between near the conical intersection of the surfaces.

Through the text, the author uses the term “Chemical space”, although in a misleading way. Indeed, this term is often used to indicate a space that composes all the possible chemical compositions of molecules, while in the manuscript is used to indicate different geometrical conformations of the same system. As one of the limitations of the approach is indeed the incapability to transfer the training obtained for a system to another one, a rephrase of the term chemical space is advised.

1. The manuscript contains a number of inaccuracies, both, pertaining to language and notation, for example:
- Page 8: “realistic chemistry on the exact electronic surface” – the term exact electronic surface typically refers to converged data with respect to electronic structure method and basis set, but the presented data are for a limited basis set and probably far from convergence. The problems of simulations are somehow acknowledged on page 9 (l. 497-503), but not reflected in the section heading.
- Page 9: “interpolating across ab initio potential energy surfaces” implies multiple potential energy surfaces, like excited states which are not covered in the manuscript.
- Page 9, eq. 8: the R-dependence of 2-ERI. Similarly, eq.9 drops the R-dependence of K and 2-ERI, and E_nuc(R) appears in eq. 5.6 but not in eq. 9 where it is explained. Such inaccuracies are particularly unfortunate, as the method is argued to benefit from the R-independent \(\Gamma_{	ext{Gamma_ab}}^{ij|kl}\) used to interpolate between geometries (see below 3.)

The paper draws broad conclusions about NQE importance for "heterogeneous catalysis" and "zeolite catalysis" based on a study of a single zeolite framework (H-CHA) with a single Si/Al ratio (35) and isolated acid sites, while the transferability tests conducted do not include proton hopping validation. "These proof-of-concept results open the window to include in a more systematic way NQEs for heterogeneous catalyzed processes." The paper frames its findings as broadly applicable to heterogeneous catalysis. However, proton hopping kinetics can vary significantly between zeolite frameworks due to differences in local geometry, confinement effects, and Si-O-Al bond angles. For instance, as shown in Table S1 of the supplementary information, experimental activation energies range from 11 to 126 kJ/mol across different zeolites (MFI, FAU, MOR, CHA), suggesting framework-dependent behavior that cannot be extrapolated from a single system. "To broaden our scope, it is important to assess the MLP capability of describing other zeolite frameworks on which the MLP was not trained... The CHA topology was included as a control system, to ensure that the MLP is robust with respect to changes in the unit cell volume." The transferability tests (Section S9) use all-silica zeolite frameworks (AFX, FER, MFI, MOR) without proton hopping validation. The paper acknowledges: "explicitly testing the proton hopping transferability would require additional expensive DFT US simulations and was therefore not further investigated." This means the key conclusion—that NQEs significantly impact proton hopping up to 473K—is demonstrated only for one specific system and may not generalize to other zeolites commonly used in catalysis. "It appears therefore that for zeolite-catalyzed processes conducted at milder conditions, among which the ones related to biomass conversion are a predominant example, NQEs might be non-negligible when computing the kinetics of proton-transfer steps." This conclusion extends findings from H-CHA proton hopping to "zeolite-catalyzed processes" broadly. However, biomass conversion typically involves additional species (water, alcohols, organic molecules) that interact with the acid site and may modulate NQE contributions differently than the isolated proton hopping studied here. The generalization to complex catalytic environments lacks supporting evidence.

This paper’s conclusions about MLP transferability beyond H-CHA (main text Discussion and Supplementary Section S9) are not adequately supported for the target phenomenon (proton hopping kinetics) because the transferability tests are performed only on all-silica frameworks without hydrogen and without Brønsted acid sites; this weakens the robustness and significance of claims about extending the approach to other zeolite topologies. “To broaden our scope, it is important to assess the MLP capability of describing other zeolite frameworks on which the MLP was not trained. To this end, the transferability of the MLP to other zeolite topologies was investigated.” The manuscript frames transferability as a key step toward broader applicability beyond the specific H-CHA case study, which is central to the work’s significance claim as a proof-of-concept for heterogeneous catalysis. However, “transferability” is only meaningful relative to the target tasks—here, reactive proton hopping around an Al defect with NQEs. If the performed validation does not include that reactive chemical environment, it cannot substantiate transferability for the main scientific quantity of interest (reaction kinetics). This matters because the conclusions invite readers to believe that only limited additional training is needed for other frameworks, which is a strong practical implication. Without reactive-site tests, the presented evidence only supports that the MLP is numerically stable on related silica frameworks, not that it is accurate for proton transfer barriers, recrossing factors, or tunneling-sensitive dynamics. As such, the transferability component does not robustly support the paper’s broader generalization. “To obtain an indication of the capability of the MLP to model other zeolite frameworks, the all-silica structures of CHA, AFX, FER, MFI and MOR were obtained from the IZA zeolite database [55].” This sentence makes explicit that the transferability test systems are all-silica frameworks, which exclude both the Al substitution and the Brønsted proton central to the paper’s chemistry. Because the MLP is trained to reproduce a reactive PES involving O–H bond breaking/forming near Al, evaluating it only on Si–O–Si network dynamics does not probe whether it generalizes to different acid-site geometries or proton-binding motifs. This is particularly important because proton transfer rates can be highly sensitive to local electrostatics and short-range interactions, not merely to the stability of the silica lattice. Therefore, while the test may demonstrate that the potential is not catastrophically unstable outside its training topology, it does not validate the claimed capability to model proton hopping in other zeolites. The manuscript’s broader implications would require at least some reactive-site test cases (even a small number) to be convincing. Without them, the conclusions about applicability beyond H-CHA are substantially overstated relative to the evidence. “Because of the lack of hydrogen atoms, the time step was increased to 1 fs.” This explicit acknowledgment confirms that hydrogen is absent in the transferability simulations, which means the validation does not cover the very degrees of freedom for which NQEs and tunneling are being claimed as critical. Since the paper’s main message is about NQEs on proton hopping kinetics, excluding hydrogen from the transferability tests disconnects the validation from the core phenomenon. It also means the test cannot reveal whether the MLP’s learned description of O–H interactions extrapolates sensibly in other frameworks or near different T-site environments. Moreover, a potential can behave “reasonably physical” for a silica lattice while being substantially wrong for reactive proton dynamics, because these are different regions of configuration space and involve different interaction scales. Consequently, the reported “moderate” force errors on silica atoms cannot be used as evidence that proton hopping kinetics would be reliable elsewhere. This limits the interpretability of the transferability section as support for broader catalytic applications. “Unfortunately, explicitly testing the proton hopping transferability would require additional expensive DFT US simulations and was therefore not further investigated.” This sentence concedes that the work does not actually test transferability for the proton hopping reaction itself, which is the central scientific target. While it is reasonable that additional DFT-US data is costly, the absence of such tests means that any transferability implication for reactive kinetics remains speculative. Because the paper positions the approach as opening a “window” to systematic inclusion of NQEs in heterogeneous catalysis, the strength of that claim depends on whether one can expect the trained MLP to generalize across frameworks or whether each new system requires substantial retraining and validation. The text provides no quantitative evidence (e.g., reactive barriers, proton localization energetics, or NQE-sensitive rate factors) in other topologies to support the implication. As a result, the conclusions about extending to other zeolite frameworks are not adequately supported by the presented validation. This affects the perceived significance and generality of the methodology.

The practical significance of the findings for zeolite catalysis is not clearly demonstrated, as the "significant impact" of NQEs is claimed only for temperatures up to ~473 K, which is below the typical operating range for many zeolite-catalyzed reactions. "NQEs are found to significantly impact the proton hopping kinetics up to ~473 K." (from Main Paper, Abstract) The authors highlight the significance of NQEs up to 473 K. However, many industrial zeolite processes, such as the Methanol-to-Olefins (MTO) conversion, operate at temperatures significantly higher (e.g., >600 K). "While the work performed here is illustrative for the most basic proton hopping reaction in zeolites, it paves the way towards a routine inclusion of NQEs..." (from Main Paper, Introduction) While the methodological contribution is noted, the specific scientific conclusion regarding the impact of NQEs on proton hopping appears to be of limited relevance for actual catalytic conditions if the effect vanishes at operating temperatures. The manuscript would benefit from a more explicit discussion of the magnitude of NQEs at these higher temperatures to contextualize the significance of the results.
The reason for the high temperature leading to large barrier (as shown in Figure 3 and Figures 15-16) should be clarified properly.
Figure S3, the “H-SSZ-13” is inconsistent with that shown in other places.

Data availability: this work has amassed a large amount of DFT data, these should be publicly shared and documented well, not only be made available upon request! The same goes for the trained potential files and all input files. Much of the progress achieved in this work would be put to waste if these data are not published.

The paper's title and abstract claim the model predicts TS structures for "general chemical reactions" and "general organic reactions," but the training and evaluation exclusively use the Grambow dataset which contains only gas-phase unimolecular reactions with small molecules (up to 7 heavy atoms, CHNO only). This represents a narrow subset of organic chemistry that does not support the generalization claims. * Quote (Title and Abstract): "Prediction of Transition State Structures of General Chemical Reactions via Machine Learning" and "Herein, a novel machine learning (ML) model for predicting TS structures of general organic reactions is proposed." The term "general" strongly implies broad applicability across diverse reaction types, molecular sizes, and chemical systems. However, the model was trained and evaluated exclusively on unimolecular gas-phase reactions with molecules containing at most 7 heavy atoms. This is a highly restricted chemical space that excludes bimolecular reactions, solution-phase chemistry, larger molecules common in pharmaceutical and industrial applications, and elements beyond C, H, N, and O. "The proposed model is trained and validated using the organic reaction database released by Grambow et al." The Grambow database, as described in the original publication, contains reactions generated from molecules selected from GDB-7 (molecules with at most 7 heavy atoms). The database specifically states "All reactions are gas-phase calculations involving up to seven carbon, oxygen, or nitrogen atoms per molecule." This severely limits the chemical diversity and molecular complexity represented in the training data. * Quote from Grambow et al. [[1]](#citation-list): "We only considered molecules with at most seven heavy atoms (C, N, O). All molecules with six or fewer heavy atoms were selected from GDB-7 (~770) and a random selection of ~430 molecules were selected from the set with seven heavy atoms." The original dataset paper explicitly acknowledges the small molecule limitation. Drug molecules, natural products, and industrial chemicals typically contain 20-50 or more heavy atoms and include elements like halogens, sulfur, and phosphorus. Without evaluation on such systems, claims about "general" chemical reactions are not substantiated. * Quote from TSNet paper (Jackson et al.) [[2]](#citation-list): "TSNet is presented, a new end-to-end Siamese message-passing neural network based on tensor field networks shown to be capable of predicting transition state geometries. Also presented is a small dataset of SN2 reactions which includes transition state structures." Even the comparison method TSNet recognized the need to evaluate on different reaction types (SN2 reactions) beyond the Grambow dataset. The present paper does not demonstrate generalization to out-of-distribution reaction types or larger molecules, yet makes stronger claims about generality. References: [1] [Grambow, C.A., Pattanaik, L. & Green, W.H. Reactants, products, and transition states of elementary chemical reactions based on quantum chemistry. Sci Data 7, 137 (2020).](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41597-020-0460-4) [2] [Jackson, R., Zhang, W. & Pearson, J. TSNet: predicting transition state structures with tensor field networks and transfer learning. Chem. Sci. 12, 10022–10040 (2021).](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2021/sc/d1sc01206a)

This paper makes an overgeneralized methodological claim (Validity, Conclusions) that the approach “can be extended to all types of chemical reactions” and “any type of element,” but the demonstrated training/validation domain is limited to small organic reactions and a narrow element set, so the stated scope is not supported by the evidence presented. “To achieve high accuracy with the satisfaction of various necessary invariances, the ML model contained PSI layers designed to handle any size of molecules and any type of element. This layer relies not on any prior knowledge of chemical reaction type but on reactant and product structures, thus can be extended to all types of chemical reactions without modifications.” This passage is a strong claim about universality across both molecular size and element types, extending beyond the experiments reported in the paper. Demonstrating size-extensivity or permutation invariance does not by itself demonstrate accuracy across arbitrarily large molecules, transition-metal chemistry, charged states, or elements not present in training. In chemical ML, “can be extended” typically requires either training on representative chemistry or explicit experiments showing transfer to new element sets or reaction classes. The paper does not provide such out-of-domain experiments or ablations demonstrating that embeddings/generalization remain reliable when introducing unseen elements. Therefore, the claim is not supported by the presented empirical evidence and risks misleading readers about the method’s validated scope. “In this study, two publicly available reaction databases are used. The first was released by Grambow et al.[28] … ωB97X-D3/def2-TZVP results (11961 reactions) … The second database includes 53 S_N2 reactions …” This sentence defines the effective experimental domain of the work: a single large dataset derived from a specific automated reaction-discovery pipeline and a very small SN2 set. These two datasets do not span “all types of chemical reactions” in the usual sense (e.g., multi-step mechanisms, organometallic catalysis, ionic and solvent-mediated reactions, excited states, etc.). The small SN2 dataset also limits the strength of any claims about generalization across reaction mechanisms. Because the paper ties broad scope to model design rather than to demonstrated transfer, the empirical support is not commensurate with the breadth of the claim. A more accurate conclusion would restrict the claim to the element/reaction types present in these datasets. “we used automated potential energy surface exploration to generate 12,000 organic reactions involving H, C, N, and O atoms calculated at the ωB97X-D3/def2-TZVP quantum chemistry level.” [1](#ref1) This establishes that the primary dataset used by the reviewed paper is restricted to a limited element set (H/C/N/O) and a specific computational level of theory. If the training data do not include other common elements in organic chemistry (e.g., halogens, sulfur, phosphorus) or any transition metals, the paper’s claim of “any type of element” cannot be empirically supported by the reported experiments. Generalization to unseen elements is nontrivial because learned embeddings and distance distributions can shift substantially when element types change. Therefore, the broad applicability claim conflicts with the scope of the dataset used for training and testing. At minimum, the paper would need separate evaluation on datasets with broader element coverage to justify such statements. “Because of the unfavorable scaling of quantum chemical calculations, we only considered molecules with at most seven heavy atoms (C, N, O).” [1](#ref1) This dataset design choice sharply constrains molecular size and composition, which is directly relevant to the reviewed paper’s “any size of molecules” claim. Even if the model architecture is formally size-extensive, accuracy and stability of predictions can degrade with increasing size due to long-range distance distributions, conformational complexity, and combinatorial growth in pairwise interactions. Without evaluation on larger molecules, it is not justified to claim practical applicability to “any size” TS structures. This mismatch between stated scope and demonstrated domain affects how readers should interpret the significance and usability of the method. It also suggests that additional experiments are needed before making general claims about scaling.

The use of a random split for the dataset likely leads to data leakage, overestimating the model's generalization capability, as the dataset contains chemically similar reactions generated by automated exploration. "The database, is split randomly into training, validation, and test sets with an 80-10-10 ratio." The paper explicitly states that a random split was used. In chemical datasets, random splitting is known to be problematic because it places structurally similar molecules or reactions (e.g., conformers or analogs) in both the training and test sets. "The first was released by Grambow et al.[28]... 11961 reactions are employed" The Grambow et al. dataset (ReactionData) consists of elementary reactions generated by automated exploration algorithms (like GSM). These algorithms often generate clusters of very similar reactions (e.g., the same reaction template applied to slightly different substrates). "Reactants, products, and transition states of elementary chemical reactions based on quantum chemistry" [1] The nature of the dataset implies high redundancy. By using a random split, the model can achieve high accuracy by memorizing the local manifold of reaction types seen during training, rather than learning generalizable chemical rules. A scaffold split or reaction-type split is required to properly evaluate the model's ability to generalize to new reactions. References: [1] Grambow, C.A., Pattanaik, L., Green, W.H.: Reactants, products, and transition states of elementary chemical reactions based on quantum chemistry. Scientific Data 7(1), 1–8 (2020). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-0460-4

As this is being machine learning approach, its performance/accuracy scales with the size of the training set. Although dataset of Grambow et al. has vast number of reactions, real applications always involve building up their own reaction data and this is even more stipulated by the failure case given in this manuscript. In this work training-validation-test set as splited into 8-1-1 ratio, but would be interesting to present performance improvement with respect to number of training set (learning curve). Then readers might be able to expect approximated accuracy when trained with this model.
In page 10, “challenging but demanding work” is awkward expression.
Unfortunately the grammar of the manuscript needs a lot of work. I trust that the Springer/Nature editorial team can work with the author to revise. I spotted no less than 20 errors.

Kinetic modeling is a visual science, but the reader sees very few examples of the model’s predictions. I recommend an additional figure, either in the main text or the supplement, showing examples of the model’s predictions--both good and poor. Including both unimolecular and bimolecular reactions would be nice. This will help the reader gain confidence in the model’s ability and may highlight areas for improvement.

There are few key limitations the author should highlight. First, that the reactant and product geometries used to generate TS guesses in this study were obtained from the Grambow dataset and are hence QM-optimized reactants and products. Thus, the model still requires QM-optimized reactants and products as input, which should be clearly stated in the manuscript. Second and more importantly, for any bimolecular reactions, the reactants and products need to be aligned in a reacting configuration, which is inherently the case in the Grambow dataset because the data were generated using the growing string method. A typical workflow performs opt-freq calculations on each species independently as traditional TST calculations expect partitions functions for each species rather than for the VDW complex that is found in the Grambow dataset. For reactions that have 2 or 3 products, which represent about 30% of this dataset, it would normally require another step to align these species to create the complex that was used to train the model presented here. Without a robust alignment scheme, it is unclear whether or not the presented model can be successfully applied to bimolecular reactions. This limitation should also be clearly stated in the paper.

The paper presents all benchmark results (Tables I-VI) as single point estimates without any error bars, standard deviations, or confidence intervals from multiple training runs, making it impossible to assess the statistical significance of the claimed performance improvements over competing methods. "TABLE I: MAE of force components on the MD-17 data set, trained on 1,000 configurations, forces in units of [meV/Å]. For the benzene molecule, two different data set exists from [17, 31] with different levels of accuracy in the DFT reference data." This table presents single MAE values for NequIP and comparison methods (SchNet, DimeNet, sGDML, FCHL19/GPR) without any uncertainty measures. Given that neural network training involves stochastic elements such as random weight initialization, data shuffling, and potentially different train/validation splits, the actual performance can vary significantly between runs. Without error bars from multiple independent training runs with different random seeds, it is impossible to determine whether the reported differences between methods (e.g., NequIP's 11.2 vs FCHL19/GPR's 11.2 meV/Å for Malonaldehyde) are statistically significant or within natural variance. "We train a series of networks with identical hyperparameters, but vary the training set sizes between 10 and 1,000 structures, sampled uniformly from the full data set, as well as a validation set consisting of 100 structures." Even for the ablation study in Figure 7 comparing l=0 vs l=0/l=1 networks, the paper does not report error bars despite the learning curves being critical evidence for the claimed data efficiency advantage of equivariant features. The figure shows smooth curves without any indication of variance across runs, which is inconsistent with typical neural network training behavior where different random seeds can lead to noticeable performance variations. "We measure the Li diffusivity in ten Nequip-driven MD simulations (computed via the slope of the mean square displacement), all of length 50 ps and started from different initial velocities, randomly sampled from a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. We find a mean diffusivity of 1.42 × 10⁻⁵ cm²/s" While the paper runs ten MD simulations for diffusivity measurements, it only reports the mean value without the standard deviation across these ten runs. This is a missed opportunity to demonstrate statistical reproducibility. Additionally, this approach of running multiple simulations is not applied to the model training itself—no information is provided about variance in model performance when trained with different random seeds. "For each task, we trained four independent models using different random seeds and report the mean and standard deviation of these independent runs" [[1]](#citation-list) In contrast to this paper, the published version in Nature Communications includes error bars and multiple training runs, following the best practices established in contemporary machine learning benchmarking. The preprint version under review lacks this critical statistical rigor that is necessary to validate the claimed improvements. References: [1] Batzner, S. et al. "E(3)-equivariant graph neural networks for data-efficient and accurate interatomic potentials." [Nature Communications 13, 2453 (2022)](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-29939-5)

This paper’s headline conclusions about extreme data efficiency and superiority over prior ML interatomic potentials are not robust because key comparisons (notably the “1000× fewer data” DeepMD comparison) are evaluated on highly correlated MD trajectory frames with train/test splits that can leak near-duplicate configurations, undermining the validity of the reported “orders-of-magnitude” gains. "NequIP outperforms existing models with up to three orders of magnitude fewer training data, challenging the widely held belief that deep neural networks require massive training sets." This is the central, strongest quantitative claim in the abstract and it materially affects the paper’s conclusions about data efficiency. Because it is a comparative statement (“outperforms existing models”), it requires a tightly controlled evaluation protocol that rules out confounders. In MD-generated datasets, adjacent frames can be extremely similar, so “randomly sampled” training frames can have near-neighbors in the test set even if they are formally disjoint. If such leakage occurs, test errors can substantially underestimate true generalization error for deployment (e.g., new thermodynamic conditions or different trajectories). The paper does not establish that its orders-of-magnitude comparisons are robust to these well-known correlation/leakage effects. "To assess data efficiency of the NequIP architecture, we similarly train a model jointly on all four parts of the data set, but using only 133 structures for training, i.e. 1000x fewer data. The 133 structures were sampled uniformly from the full data set available online, consisting of water and ice structures, made up of a total of 140,000 frames, coming from the same MD trajectories that were used in the earlier work [15]. We also use a validation set of 50 frames and report the test accuracy on all remaining structures in the data set. ... We find that with 1000x fewer training data, NequIP significantly outperforms DeepMD on all four parts of the data set." The protocol explicitly samples training/validation frames from the same MD trajectories and then evaluates on “all remaining” frames, which is exactly the situation where temporal correlation can make held-out frames very similar to training frames. That similarity can make interpolation look like strong generalization, especially when the test set contains many near-duplicates of training points. The DeepMD numbers are attributed to a model “previously trained [15]”, but the paper does not specify whether DeepMD was evaluated on exactly the same held-out frames and with the same metric definition, leaving comparability unclear. Since the conclusion hinges on a 1000× data reduction, even modest evaluation mismatches or leakage can qualitatively change the interpretation. As written, the evidence does not justify that the reported 1000× advantage reflects a robust, apples-to-apples generalization improvement. "However, if the data has many similar conditions, e.g., as occurs for data generated from AIMD trajectories or small perturbations to existing structures, then these random CV approaches will yield overly optimistic predictions. The predictions will be overly optimistic due to the “twin” problem, where extremely similar data is present in both the train and test sets, and the model predictions are thus indicative of data that looks just like the training data." ([1](#citation-list)) This directly describes the failure mode of using random splits on trajectory data: even without explicit leakage, near-duplicate configurations across train/test can yield overly optimistic errors. The paper’s water/ice comparison and several other experiments explicitly sample “uniformly” from MD frames and evaluate on the remaining frames from the same trajectories, matching the scenario warned about here. Because the paper’s main scientific claim is exceptional data efficiency, any optimism bias in the evaluation can inflate the apparent sample-efficiency advantage. The quoted guidance also clarifies what more robust alternatives look like (e.g., testing on new conditions or later trajectory segments), which the paper does not consistently implement for its headline “orders-of-magnitude” comparisons. Therefore, this literature evidence supports that the reported gains may not reflect robust out-of-sample performance. "We show that the proposed scheme provides an efficient and accurate protocol in a variety of systems, including bulk materials and molecules. In all these cases, DPMD gives results that are essentially indistinguishable from the original data, at a cost that scales linearly with system size." ([4](#citation-list)) This highlights that DeepMD (the principal baseline used for the 1000× claim) is itself presented in the literature as an accurate, symmetry-preserving approach trained on ab initio labels. That makes the paper’s claim of “significantly outperforms DeepMD” with 1000× less data unusually strong, and therefore it warrants especially careful, transparent, and matched evaluation. When a baseline is described as reproducing “results ... essentially indistinguishable from the original data,” modest differences in dataset composition, splitting, and metric definitions can dominate apparent gaps. The paper does not provide enough detail to verify that the DeepMD and NequIP results were produced under identical test conditions. As a result, the conclusion that NequIP fundamentally changes the data requirement relative to DeepMD is not convincingly supported.

The evaluation on complex material systems (Formate on Cu and Li4P2O7) lacks comparison with other ML-IP baselines, making it difficult to contextualize the performance and verify the claim of "state-of-the-art accuracy" for these specific systems. "The MAE of the predicted forces using a NequIP model trained on 2,500 structures is shown in Table IV demonstrating that NequIP is able to accurately model the interatomic forces for this complex reactive system." (Heterogeneous catalysis of formate dehydrogenation section) Table IV presents only NequIP's performance. Without comparing to other methods (e.g., SchNet, DeepMD) on the same dataset, it is impossible to determine if this performance is truly "state-of-the-art" or if simpler models could achieve similar accuracy. The paper claims to be accurate "compared to existing ML-IP methods across a wide variety of systems" (Introduction), but this comparison is absent for the material systems. "Table V shows the test set error in the force components on both the test set from the AIMD molten trajectory and the full AIMD quenched glass trajectory." (Lithium Phosphate Amorphous Glass Formation section) Similarly, Table V only reports NequIP's results. The lack of baselines weakens the argument that NequIP is uniquely capable of handling these complex systems compared to existing approaches.

Regarding the comparison between the dynamics using AIMD and NequIP-MD, Fig. 5 shows a really nice agreement. Just as a sanity check, I would add a simulation of NequIP-MD for a longer simulation time (500 ps, this should not be a problem for a ML model since they usually take a couple of ms per prediction as shown in Table VII) just to be sure that the simulation remains stable and reliable. Then report its g(r) in Fig. 5 as comparison. I understand that they could differ a bit.

The biggest concern is the fact that the energy part of the model is missing, even though the main predictor of the network is indeed the energy (eq. 3). Furthermore, total energy analysis is a standard measure of accuracy in the field, hence this shows that the work is not complete.

Although it is very impressive that the authors are able to train a MLP for water with 1000x fewer training data compared to DeepMD, bear in mind that the first generation of DeepMD (the un-smooth version) is highly data-inefficient due to the \"cusps\" introduced by the permutations of atoms. Also the water data set contains the whole MD trajectory, meaning that the training data is highly correlated so significant sparsification can be done before hampering the amount of information contained in the data set. I suggest that the authors consider including these more nuanced points, so that readers who are not familiar with the field won't be misled.

The genotoxicity assessment was performed only for the 11.7T group using before/after intra-subject comparisons without a 0T control group, which is methodologically inconsistent with how other safety measures were evaluated and may be insufficient given that prior literature has demonstrated genotoxic effects from repeated MRI exposure. "Genotoxicity was also assessed but only for the 11.7T group with blood samples drawn before and after the 90 min exposure to perform intra-subject comparisons." While the behavioral, vestibular, and physiological tests included both 11.7T and 0T control groups for between-group comparisons, the genotoxicity assessment inexplicably omits the 0T control group. This creates an asymmetry in the study design where arguably the most concerning potential safety outcome (genetic damage) receives less rigorous evaluation than other measures. An intra-subject pre/post design without a control group cannot account for time-dependent changes or procedural effects unrelated to magnetic field exposure. "No statistically significant differences in micronuclei count for genotoxicity for each subject before versus after exposure at 11.7T were found either." The absence of statistical significance in micronuclei counts from a single 90-minute exposure in 20 subjects has limited interpretability. Reference [15] cited in the paper (Herate et al., 2022 [[4]](#citation-4)) specifically examined effects of "repeated brain MRI" at 3T. The current study's single-exposure design may be insufficient to detect cumulative genotoxic effects that could emerge with the repeated exposures typical of clinical or research MRI use. The authors cite this reference but do not adequately address why single-exposure testing is sufficient given the documented effects from repeated exposures. "Having shown that the repetitive exposure to 3T in MRI [15] induces mainly terminal deletions, the micronuclei protocol was performed with cytochalasin B addition at 68h after lymphocyte growth stimulation" The authors acknowledge that their reference [15] demonstrated genotoxic effects from repeated MRI exposure. However, the study design tests only single exposure, which does not address whether repeated 11.7T exposures would produce similar or enhanced effects. Given that chronic or repeated exposure has been identified as the relevant exposure scenario for genotoxicity concerns, the single-exposure design represents a significant gap in the safety assessment. References: [4] Herate, C., Brochard, P., De Vathaire, F., et al. "The effects of repeated brain MRI on chromosomal damage." European Radiology Experimental 6, 12 (2022). https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s41747-022-00264-2

The manuscript’s methodological reporting is insufficient to evaluate whether the claimed SAR mitigation and pTx safety control are robust across subjects, because key safety-relevant quantities (e.g., achieved SAR margins, hotspot validation, and sensitivity to motion) are not presented for the 11.7T pTx protocols. "We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate." This statement links the main technical contribution (pTx) to a core human safety requirement (SAR control), but it is presented as a high-level assertion without the quantitative details necessary for independent evaluation. At ultra-high field, SAR limitations and local hotspots are among the primary safety constraints, so a reader needs to see at least the operational SAR limits used, how conservatism was set, and how compliance was verified in vivo. The paper later mentions virtual observation points and constrained pulse design, but does not provide the actual SAR values (global/head SAR and local 10g SAR), margins to limits, or per-subject distributions that would substantiate “tamed” SAR. Because the safety conclusion depends on this control being reliable for all scanned subjects, the lack of quantitative reporting undermines the credibility of the safety claim. This is a methodological transparency gap that directly affects validity. "The GRAPE pulse was a universal RF pulse [28] and was designed offline on a database constituted of the 6 first subject field maps." A universal pulse designed from a limited database can be practical, but it raises two safety-relevant questions: how robust is it to out-of-distribution anatomy/positioning, and how conservative are the SAR constraints under variability. The manuscript does not report any validation results showing that the universal pulse maintained SAR compliance and flip-angle targets across the remaining subjects beyond the six used to design it. In pTx, performance robustness and SAR robustness are not guaranteed to coincide; a pulse can appear to work (acceptable flip-angle error) while still creating higher local SAR hotspots in some anatomies. Without reporting robustness statistics or worst-case analyses, readers cannot judge whether the approach generalizes safely beyond the design set. This is particularly important because the paper’s conclusions generalize to “humans” rather than to the six-map database. "It enables spatial field manipulation, but at the same time, it may create hotspots by focusing the RF fields. This means that accurate subject-specific SAR measurement is mandatory to operate pTx appropriately." [[1]](#cite-1) This safety review summarizes a widely accepted concern in high-field pTx: hotspot creation is an intrinsic risk, and safe operation depends on accurate (often subject-specific) SAR assessment. The reviewed paper does not report subject-specific SAR verification, nor does it describe any empirical validation (e.g., temperature monitoring or phantom verification) that would allow independent assessment of SAR modeling accuracy at 11.7T. While the paper mentions virtual observation points, it does not show how conservative the VOP set was, what head models were used, or whether model mismatch was accounted for. Given the “mandatory” nature of accurate SAR assessment emphasized in the literature, omission of these details is a major methodological gap rather than a minor reporting choice. Consequently, the reader cannot verify that the pTx approach was safely controlled across participants. "They compared conventional quadrature excitation and multi-spoke parallel-transmit excitation after RF shimming and found that the local SAR increased by more than 100% in 1 and 4 cm3 for quadrature and parallel-transmit excitation, respectively, in the worst case where the head model was shifted by 20 and 10 mm in right and posterior directions, respectively." [[1]](#cite-1) This quoted finding highlights that relatively plausible head-position changes can dramatically increase local SAR under pTx, which is directly relevant for a 90-minute session where motion is likely. The reviewed paper acknowledges motion problems for image quality but does not connect motion to SAR safety, and it does not report how head stabilization and motion were handled from a safety standpoint. Without a motion-sensitivity analysis or conservative SAR margins that explicitly account for potential motion, the claim that SAR was “tamed” is not fully supported. In ultra-high-field first-in-human studies, demonstrating safety robustness to realistic deviations (including motion) is an important part of safety reporting. The absence of this analysis reduces confidence that the protocol is safe beyond idealized conditions. References: [1] Okada T, Akasaka T, Thuy DHD, Isa T. *Safety for Human MR Scanners at 7T*. Magnetic Resonance in Medical Sciences (2022). https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9618930/

The provided code repository only contains scripts for the behavioral task (ANT), while the core technical innovation (pTx pulse design and reconstruction) is not shared. "We tackled this problem by deploying parallel transmission hardware and software..." The paper attributes the success of the imaging to this software, yet it is not provided. ``` ANT-R task ============= ... `ant-r-classic.py` is a script implementing the ANT-R task described in Fan, J. et al (2009). ``` The `README.md` in the provided code repository confirms that the code is exclusively for the "Attentional Network Task" (behavioral), not for the MRI pulse design or image reconstruction. "The code repository... contains several Python scripts implementing variants of the ANT task." The absence of the imaging code prevents reproduction of the pTx pulses and image reconstruction, which are the primary technical contributions enabling the 11.7T images.
Show images of all subjects, e.g. in the supplementary materials, to demonstrate the robustness of results or at least discuss issues with robustness at this stage.
I miss a clearer and more detailed discussion about what the current results and future developments mean for scientific/neuroscience and potentially clinical applications.

In Fig 2 it is unclear which exact conditions were used in each of the 3/7/11.7 T measurements. Would they be expected to only vary in resolution (i.e. were TE/TR adjusted for the different field T2(*/T1) or were they performed under the same conditions (TE/TR)? If the latter, contrast is expected to vary, and to make a good comparison it is recommended to match the contrast conditions while only varying resolution if possible. Please clarify.

The systematic uncertainty budget in Table I lists several contributions as upper bounds (e.g., "<0.16 MHz" for MW-Beam alignment, "<0.07 MHz" for MW field intensity) without providing the derivations or methods used to establish these bounds. For a precision measurement intended to guide future experiments, the methodology for estimating systematic uncertainties should be more thoroughly documented. "MW-Beam alignment: <0.16; MW field intensity: <0.07; M velocity distribution: <0.01; AC Stark 2P₃/₂: +0.39, <0.02; 2nd-order Doppler: +0.03, <0.01; Earth's Field: <0.05; Quantum Interference: <0.04" Table I presents seven systematic contributions as upper bounds, but the main text provides limited detail on how these bounds were determined. While the total systematic uncertainty is small compared to the 6.8 MHz statistical uncertainty (making detailed systematic analysis less urgent for this measurement), future precision measurements targeting the ~kHz level mentioned in the paper would require rigorous characterization of these effects. For example, the MW-beam alignment uncertainty of <0.16 MHz is the largest systematic, yet no description is provided of how the alignment was verified or how this bound was derived. Similarly, the quantum interference contribution of <0.04 MHz references established effects from hydrogen spectroscopy but does not detail the calculation for muonium. "The AC Stark shift due to the coupling to the 2P₃/₂ states is calculated for the average atom to be +0.39 MHz. [...] The 2nd order Doppler shift is +0.03 MHz." While the AC Stark and Doppler shifts are given as specific values, the uncertainties on these shifts are again presented only as upper bounds (<0.02 and <0.01 MHz respectively). For a comprehensive error budget, the uncertainties should ideally be derived from the uncertainties in the underlying parameters (microwave power, beam velocity distribution, etc.) rather than stated as bounds without justification. This is particularly relevant because the paper envisions future measurements at the kHz level where these systematics would become dominant.

The claim of observing the Muonium 3S state is not supported by the statistical significance of the data. "We also present the first observation of Muonium in the n = 3 excited state." The authors claim to have observed the 3S state for the first time. However, in particle physics and precision spectroscopy, an "observation" typically requires a statistical significance of at least 5 sigma. "Chi^2 ... 6.7 ... 2.0" (from the table in the text) The improvement in Chi-squared when including the 3S component is only 4.7 (from 6.7 to 2.0). For one additional degree of freedom, this corresponds to a significance of roughly 2.1 sigma. Furthermore, the Chi-squared of 6.7 for the fit *without* the 3S component (with 5 degrees of freedom) corresponds to a p-value of approximately 0.24, indicating that the null hypothesis (no 3S state) provides an acceptable fit to the data. Therefore, the data does not statistically require the presence of the 3S state, making the claim of "observation" an overstatement.

What is Authors’ definition of the Lamb shift ?, this should be explicitly written somewhere, Namely it is the difference in the centroid values of 2S_1/2 and 2P_1/2 levels. This is important in a view of significant mixing between P_1/2 and P_3/2 states.

This paper describes results following the n=2 Lamb shift spectroscopy of muonium reported by the group at Physical Review Letters this year (B. Ohayon et al., “Precision Measurement of the Lamb Shift in Muonium,” Phys. Rev. Lett., 128, 011802 (2022), hereinafter referred to as Oyahon2022.). It is possible that this paper could be interpreted as an independent confirmation of the results reported in Oyahon2022, in which case it is difficult to determine from this manuscript whether the level of novelty and significance required by the journal has been satisfied.

[Introduction section] The reviewer understands that the Barker-Glover effect is part of the recoil correction, but what is the significance of being able to verify this as a test for bound-state QED?

[Experiment section] If the events in region A are derived from convoy electrons or ionization products, can we distinguish them from the signal by using the difference in waveform or pulse height of the MCP signals?

[Results section] Reduced chi-square = 2.0 has a different meaning depending on the degree of freedom. For example, p=0.08 is obtained for chi-square10 with 5 degrees of freedom, p=0.03 for chi-square20 with 10 degrees of freedom. To eliminate ambiguity, the reviewer recommends describing both chi-square and degrees of freedom instead of reduced chi-square.

[Conclusions section] Is there a possibility that n=3 spectroscopy, such as 3S-3D5/2, can be performed with muonium to gain new insights that have not been obtained before? If so, it would be good to be a little more specific.

'Results' section: It is said that reduced chi^2=6.7 and 2 (for different fits). 6.7 is definitely bad and that is why the other fir was performed. But I do not understand whether 2 is OK. It would be good to say what is the number of degrees of freedom and what is the probability that reduced chi^2 for those dof is >2.0.

This paper’s reported discovery-level statistical significance for the T\(_{cc}^+\) peak is not demonstrably valid because it is derived using Wilks’ theorem on an analysis that (i) uses sPlot subtraction with negative weights and (ii) tests a signal-yield parameter that is effectively on the boundary, both of which violate regularity assumptions needed to trust asymptotic \(\chi^2\)-based likelihood-ratio calibrations. “The statistical significance of the observed T\(_{cc}^+ \to\) D\(^0\)D\(^0\pi^+\) signal is estimated using Wilks’ theorem [94] to be 22 standard deviations.” This sentence anchors the central discovery claim on an asymptotic result (Wilks) that requires regularity conditions (e.g., parameters not on boundaries, standard likelihood construction, and well-behaved nuisance structure). In this analysis, the signal yield is intrinsically non-negative in the physical model, so the null hypothesis “no signal” places the yield at the boundary; this is a known setting where the naive Wilks \(\chi^2\) reference distribution does not hold. The problem is not merely pedantic: boundary behavior changes the null distribution of test statistics and therefore the mapping from \(-2\Delta\ln\mathcal{L}\) to “\(\sigma\)” units. Additionally, the paper’s pipeline uses a statistically-subtracted (weighted) dataset, which typically implies that the objective function is not a conventional (unweighted) log-likelihood for i.i.d. events. Without an explicit demonstration (e.g., toy-MC calibration under the full procedure), the quoted “22 standard deviations” cannot be independently trusted as a frequentist false-positive rate. Even if the true significance remains very large, the paper’s *method* for claiming it is not justified on the stated assumptions. “Each candidate is assigned a positive weight for being signal-like or a negative weight for being background-like, with the masses of the two \( D^0 \) candidates as the discriminating variables. All candidates are then retained and the weights are used in the further analysis for statistical subtraction of non-D\(^0\) background.” This describes an analysis stage that produces negative event weights and then uses them downstream, which is not the standard setting under which Wilks’ theorem is usually invoked. A conventional extended unbinned likelihood assumes contributions from events are log-probabilities of an actual sampling distribution (and expected counts are non-negative); negative weights typically correspond to an estimator for a background-subtracted distribution, not a generative likelihood. If the final fit and likelihood-ratio test treat the weighted sample “as if” it were an ordinary unweighted dataset, the resulting likelihood-ratio statistic may not have the nominal asymptotic distribution. Moreover, the variance introduced by the subtraction itself (and any correlations it induces) can distort test-statistic behavior relative to a pure maximum-likelihood fit on unsubtracted data with an explicit background component. The manuscript does not provide a calibration of the likelihood-ratio statistic under this weighted procedure. In high-stakes “observation” claims, the standard practice is to validate the test statistic’s distribution under the null with pseudoexperiments when regularity is questionable. “For the special case of \(\mu′ = 0\), this reduces to \( f(q_0|0) = \tfrac{1}{2}\delta(q_0) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{q_0}} e^{-q_0/2}.\) That is, one finds a mixture of a delta function at zero and a chi-square distribution for one degree of freedom, with each term having a weight of 1/2.” [[1]](#ref1) This statement makes explicit that, for the common discovery test in particle physics where the signal strength is constrained to be non-negative, the null distribution is *not* the standard Wilks \(\chi^2_1\) but a half-\(\chi^2\) mixture. The paper under review reports a Wilks-based “22 standard deviations” without clarifying whether such boundary corrections were applied or whether the parameter was allowed to go negative for calibration purposes. While the numerical difference at extremely high significance may be small, the paper’s claim is nevertheless not methodologically supported as written. The distinction matters because the mapping from \(q_0\) to \(Z\) is only straightforward (\(Z=\sqrt{q_0}\)) under the correct mixture distribution. More importantly, the paper’s likelihood is further complicated by background subtraction weights, so even this corrected asymptotic form may fail.
