Best practice in such nonstandard conditions is to validate via pseudoexperiments that include the full weighting/subtraction pipeline. “In these and other settings that involve testing a hypothesis that is on the boundary of the parameter space, contrary to common practice, the nominal \(\chi^2\) distribution for the LRT or the F distribution for the F test should not be used.” [[4]](#ref4) This directly contradicts the paper’s unqualified reliance on Wilks’ theorem for a signal-existence test where the null is naturally at the boundary (signal yield zero). The cited discussion emphasizes that boundary problems persist “even asymptotically” and can yield unpredictable false-positive rates unless the reference distribution is properly calibrated. In the present paper, the discovery claim is the main scientific outcome, so the calibration of its significance is central to validity. The authors could have mitigated the concern by (i) describing the exact test statistic, (ii) stating the boundary handling, and (iii) showing toy-MC calibration under the background-only hypothesis that includes sPlot subtraction. Because none of this is provided in the main paper, the numerical “22 standard deviations” is not reproducibly interpretable as a discovery-level p-value. This is a major methodological gap given field norms for claims of observation. “Strictly speaking, Wilks’ theorem does not apply here since the tested value of \(\mu\) is on the boundary of the allowed region. This difficulty however can be easily alleviated by extending the allowed region of \(\mu\) to negative values and then reducing the p-value by half.” [[2]](#ref2) This passage summarizes a standard fix used in HEP when applying likelihood-ratio tests to a non-negative signal-strength parameter. The paper under review does not state whether it applied this fix (or any equivalent correction) when quoting a Wilks-based 22\(\sigma\) significance. That omission matters because the paper presents the result as a calibrated “standard deviations” statement rather than as an internal test statistic value. The quoted fix also highlights that boundary issues are expected in exactly the type of resonance searches performed here. Given the additional complication of negative sPlot weights, simply appealing to Wilks without an explicit correction and validation is not sufficient. References: [1] [G. Cowan, K. Cranmer, E. Gross, O. Vitells, “Asymptotic formulae for likelihood-based tests of new physics” (arXiv:1007.1727)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1007.1727.pdf) [2] [E. Gross, O. Vitells, “Trial factors for the look elsewhere effect in high energy physics” (arXiv:1005.1891)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1005.1891.pdf) [4] [R. Protassov et al., “Statistics: Handle with Care: Detecting Multiple Model Components with the Likelihood Ratio Test” (arXiv:astro-ph/0201547)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/astro-ph/0201547.pdf)

The analysis assumes a two-body decay structure $T_{cc}^+ \to (2D^0) + \pi^+$ with a threshold at $2m_{D^0} + m_{\pi^+}$, which ignores the dominant intermediate state $D^{*+} D^0$ and its associated threshold behavior in the immediate vicinity of the signal. "A two-body decay structure T\(_{cc}^+ \to\) AB is assumed with \(m_A = 2m_{D^0}\) and \(m_B = m_{\pi^+}\), where \(m_{\pi^+}\) stands for the known mass of the \(\pi^+\) meson." This assumption implies that the phase space opens at $2m_{D^0} + m_{\pi^+}$, which is approximately 6 MeV below the $D^{*+} D^0$ threshold. Since the signal is located at the $D^{*+} D^0$ threshold ($\delta m \approx -273$ keV), the relevant physical threshold is $D^{*+} D^0$. By using the lower 3-body threshold, the model fails to capture the rapid variation of the phase space near the signal peak, leading to distorted line shapes. "The near-threshold mass together with a strikingly narrow width reveals the resonance nature of the state." The "resonance nature" and its parameters depend critically on the threshold behavior. Ignoring the $D^{*+}$ width and the correct threshold location (treating it as a simple 2-body decay to $2D^0 \pi$) misrepresents the underlying physics of the decay, which proceeds via $T_{cc}^+ \to D^{*+} D^0 \to D^0 D^0 \pi^+$.

The paper’s physical interpretation asserts that CP symmetry should hold in the \(\Lambda_b^0 \to R(p\pi^-)R(K^-\pi^+)\) topology because the tree amplitude does not contribute, yet the measured asymmetry in that region is non-negligible; the manuscript does not reconcile this tension (e.g., via look-elsewhere corrected significance or demonstrating consistency with residual systematics), weakening the reliability of the interpretation. "The \( \Lambda_b^0 \to R(p\pi^-)R(K^-\pi^+) \) decay involves a \( b \to d\bar{d}s \) transition, where the \( d \) quark forms the \( R(p\pi^-) \) baryon together with \( ud \) quarks from the initial \( \Lambda_b^0 \) baryon and the remaining \( \bar{d}s \) quarks forms the \( R(K^-\pi^+) \) meson. In this case, the tree amplitude does not contribute, and thus \( CP \) symmetry is expected to hold." This is a strong, testable theoretical statement embedded in the manuscript’s interpretation section. When such a topology is used as a quasi-null expectation, a measured nonzero asymmetry in that region becomes an important internal consistency check of the analysis. If the measured asymmetry is compatible with zero after accounting for multiple testing and systematic uncertainties, the paper should explicitly show that consistency to avoid confusion. Conversely, if the asymmetry remains notable, the statement CP symmetry is expected to hold may be too simplified for the selected mass window (which can still contain interfering contributions or residual tree pollution). The manuscript does not provide the quantitative reconciliation needed to support its interpretive narrative. "\( \Lambda_b^0 \to R(p\pi^-)R(K^-\pi^+) \)" and "(2.7 0.8 0.1)%" Numerically, this corresponds to a roughly 3 deviation from zero if one uses the quoted uncertainties in the same way the manuscript uses them elsewhere (i.e., dividing by the total uncertainty). Even if the authors later argue that this is not significant after accounting for the look-elsewhere effect across multiple regions, the paper does not report the corrected p-value or corrected significance for this specific region. Because the interpretation claims CP should hold here, leaving this apparent deviation unquantified is problematic: it can either indicate residual experimental bias in that region or reflect inadequacy of the simplified topology-level argument. Either way, the absence of a clear reconciliation weakens confidence in the data interpretation. Reporting the look-elsewhere corrected significance (and/or a dedicated systematic cross-check) for this null-expected region would materially improve the robustness of the conclusions. "When searching for a new resonance somewhere in a possible mass range, the significance of observing a local excess of events must take into account the probability of observing such an excess anywhere in the range. This is the so called \"look elsewhere effect\"." ([[2]](#ref2)) While Gross & Vitells discuss look-elsewhere effects for resonance searches, the underlying principle applies generally: when multiple correlated regions or selections are examined, local significances can be inflated relative to global significance. The manuscript acknowledges a look-elsewhere correction for the local \(\mathcal{A}_{CP}\) measurements, but it does not provide the corrected significance values for each region, including the topology where CP is expected to hold. Given the tension between expectation and the quoted central value, readers need the globally adjusted significance (or an explicit statement that the result becomes fully compatible with zero) to interpret this check correctly. Without this, the narrative risks appearing internally inconsistent: CP should hold is stated, yet a seemingly notable asymmetry is reported in the corresponding region. The external reference supports why the paper should explicitly quantify how multiple testing affects this apparent deviation. References: [2] [E. Gross, O. Vitells, Trial factors for the look elsewhere effect in high energy physics (arXiv:1005.1891)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1005.1891)

The paper reports a local significance of 6.0 standard deviations for the CP asymmetry in a specific phase-space region without explicitly clarifying whether this value is corrected for the Look-Elsewhere Effect (LEE) in the abstract and summary. "In particular, the $CP$ asymmetry is found to be most pronounced in the region dominated by the resonant decays $\Lambda_b^0 \to R(p\pi^+\pi^-)K^-$, measured to be $\mathcal{A}_{CP} = (5.4 \pm 0.9 \pm 0.1)\%$, which differs from zero by 6.0 standard deviations." This statement in the abstract/summary presents 6.0 standard deviations as a key result. It is unclear if this value includes the penalty for scanning multiple phase-space regions. "Subsequently, the look-elsewhere effect [63] is accounted for to correct for the increased probability of observing a significant result due to multiple measurements." While the text mentions that the LEE is accounted for, it does not explicitly link the 6.0 sigma figure in the abstract to this correction. If 6.0 sigma is the uncorrected local significance, reporting it prominently without the corrected value (or the number of trials) exaggerates the significance of the local observation. For a "first observation" paper, transparency regarding the statistical penalty of looking at specific sub-regions is crucial.

For the LiH crystal, the paper uses a HF-based finite-size correction from a 2×2×2 to extrapolate thermodynamic properties, but performs the larger 3×3×3 calculation at only a single geometry (equilibrium lattice constant) and at the Γ-point only, without twist averaging. Standard practice in QMC studies of solids typically employs twist-averaged boundary conditions (TABC) for 3D systems to reduce finite-size errors, as the authors themselves use for graphene. "For further validation, we have also computed directly the 3 × 3 × 3 supercell of LiH at its equilibrium length 4.061Å, which contains 108 electrons." The 3×3×3 calculation is performed at only a single lattice constant, making it impossible to assess how the method performs across the equation of state for this larger system. The comparison "also very close to the experimental value −4.759 eV" relies heavily on the accuracy of the HF-based finite-size correction rather than demonstrating convergence with system size through twist averaging as done for graphene. "The calculations are performed on a 2 × 2 × 2 supercell of graphene using twist average boundary condition (TABC) [25] in conjunction with structure factor S(k) correction [26] (see Supplementary Fig. 3) to reduce the finite-size error." The paper applies TABC for graphene but not for LiH, despite LiH being a 3D system where finite-size errors are typically more severe. A recent transferable neural wavefunction study [[2]](#citation-2) demonstrated that for LiH specifically, joint optimization across multiple twists and system sizes significantly reduces computational cost while improving accuracy. The absence of TABC for LiH in this work represents an inconsistency in methodology across systems. "Using our neural network, we first simulate the equation of state of LiH on a 2 × 2 × 2 supercell, as shown in Fig. 2f. In addition, we employ a standard finite-size correction based on Hartree-Fock calculations of a large supercell" The finite-size correction methodology relies on HF calculations rather than using neural network calculations at multiple supercell sizes to extrapolate to the thermodynamic limit. While this is computationally pragmatic, it introduces uncertainty about how much of the reported cohesive energy accuracy is attributable to the neural network versus the HF correction. Standard QMC practice often involves calculating multiple system sizes to directly quantify and extrapolate finite-size effects [[3]](#citation-3). References: [2] Gerard, L., Scherbela, M., Sutterud, H., Foulkes, W. M. C. & Grohs, P. Transferable neural wavefunctions for solids. [Nat. Comput. Sci. 5, 1147–1157 (2025)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43588-025-00872-z). [3] Chiesa, S., Ceperley, D. M., Martin, R. M. & Holzmann, M. Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions. [Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404 (2006)](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404).

The manuscript reports many high-precision quantitative outcomes (e.g., “within 0.1 eV/atom,” “error of less than 1%”) without clearly defining statistical uncertainties, their estimators, or how uncertainties propagate through derived metrics like cohesive energies and “correlation error,” which weakens the reliability of the conclusions. “Supplementary Figure 2 | \(2 \times 2\) Graphene training curve. For clarity, at each iteration number, we show the median energy per primitive cell over the last 10% of iteration.” Reporting a “median … over the last 10%” is a visualization choice that does not, by itself, provide a statistically interpretable uncertainty estimate for the final energies. Medians suppress outliers but do not quantify sampling variance, autocorrelation effects, or residual optimization drift, all of which are central in VMC-style estimations. Because the paper’s main claims hinge on small energy differences (sub-eV per atom) and on “outperforming” baselines, the uncertainty model needs to be explicit and standard. The paper does not accompany these median curves with defined error bars or confidence intervals. Without an explicit estimator (e.g., mean ± standard error with reblocking), a reader cannot assess whether apparent improvements exceed stochastic noise. This directly weakens confidence in the reported accuracy margins. ```html -76.15588(6) -76.24949(5) -76.26314(5) ``` The parenthetical notation strongly suggests an uncertainty is being reported, but the manuscript does not define what “(6)” or “(5)” means (e.g., one standard error of the mean, one standard deviation, or an error from fitting). In QMC practice, different conventions exist, and the distinction matters when comparing to claimed sub-0.1 eV/atom accuracy. Moreover, twist averaging and structure-factor corrections introduce additional sources of uncertainty and bias beyond the raw Monte Carlo sampling noise. The table as presented also does not state whether these uncertainties are computed after equilibration, reblocking, or accounting for autocorrelation. Without an explicit definition, the reported precision cannot be reliably interpreted. This is a significant issue because the paper repeatedly uses precise numerical comparisons to support strong conclusions. “Overall, our neural network performs very well, with an error of less than 1% in a wide range of density (see Supplementary Table 11).” This claim depends on a “correlation error” metric defined using multiple energies (HF, a DMC reference, and the network’s estimate). Such derived ratios can be sensitive to small denominators (e.g., at high density where correlation energies are small) and to uncertainties in each contributing term. The manuscript does not provide a propagation-of-uncertainty analysis for this percentage, nor does it state whether the “<1%” bound holds within statistical confidence. If the underlying energies have correlated Monte Carlo noise (common in reusing walkers, or in shared correction schemes), naive error reporting can be misleading. In addition, finite-size and fixed-node biases can dominate over sampling error for some regimes, but these are not separated. As a result, the “<1%” statement is hard to interpret as a robust quantitative guarantee. This weakens the reliability of the conclusions drawn from the HEG comparison. (excerpt below) [[1]](#ref1) ``` After network optimization, we run O(105) MCMC steps and calculate the mean local energy every 10 steps. The energy and associated standard error are estimated using a standard approach to account for correlations. 60 ``` This quote exemplifies common practice in neural-network VMC papers: energies are reported with a clearly stated “standard error” estimate that accounts for correlated samples. The reviewed manuscript does not provide a comparable definition of its parenthetical uncertainties, nor does it state how autocorrelation is handled in its estimates. Because QMC sampling is correlated by construction, omission of correlation-aware error estimation can materially understate uncertainty. The quote is not presented to require identical methods, but to illustrate the field’s baseline expectation for uncertainty reporting when making strong quantitative claims. Without similarly explicit uncertainty definitions, the reviewed paper’s precision claims are less credible. This affects both interpretability and reproducibility. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf)

The paper claims the method is "highly efficient" but provides no computational cost analysis (e.g., wall-clock time, memory usage, total FLOPs), while the reported hyperparameters suggest the method is computationally expensive. "based on which we develop a highly efficient QMC method" (Abstract) The term "highly efficient" implies a competitive computational cost relative to other methods. However, the paper provides no data to support this. Neural network ansatzes in QMC are generally known to be orders of magnitude more expensive than conventional Slater-Jastrow QMC or DFT. "Training steps 2e5... Batch size 4096" (Supplementary Table 1) The requirement of 200,000 training steps with a batch size of 4096 samples implies nearly a billion evaluations of the neural network. For a solid-state system with all-electron calculations (like LiH), this represents a massive computational burden. Without a cost comparison, the claim of "high efficiency" is misleading and likely incorrect compared to the "traditional methods" the paper claims to outperform.

The method fundamentally lacks invariance under atomic permutations, which is a critical limitation for practical applications in chemistry where identical atoms are indistinguishable, and this limitation is not adequately addressed. "This approach is invariant to translation and rigid body rotations of the chemical system, provided a consistent ordering of the SAO representation is maintained, which is straightforward to achieve. However, this is not trivial for atomic permutations or point group symmetries which would change the SAO ordering, and hence probability amplitudes of the state definition." The method's lack of permutation invariance is a fundamental limitation compared to established machine learning potentials which incorporate this symmetry by construction. For systems with chemically equivalent atoms (e.g., the hydrogen chain or the water molecule), the potential energy surface must be invariant under exchange of identical nuclei. The current method requires a "consistent ordering" which breaks this fundamental symmetry unless explicitly handled. "A molecular potential energy surface has the symmetry properties of invariance to rotation of the whole molecule, inversion of all atomic coordinates, and permutation of indistinguishable nuclei." from [Permutation invariant polynomial neural network approach, J. Chem. Phys.][4] This reference establishes that permutation invariance is a fundamental requirement for molecular potential energy surfaces. While the paper acknowledges this limitation and defers it to "future work," the lack of permutation invariance means the method in its current form cannot properly handle many chemically important scenarios such as diffusion processes involving exchange of identical atoms or reactions where atom labeling becomes ambiguous. "Furthermore, in contrast with building a force field based on local descriptors, the inference requires the same dimensionality Hilbert space for the electronic state, necessitating that the training and prediction points are taken from the same sized system." This additional limitation means the method cannot be applied to systems of different sizes, unlike transferable machine learning potentials. Combined with the lack of permutation invariance, this significantly restricts the practical applicability of the approach compared to established methods like GAP or neural network potentials that incorporate these symmetries by design. References: [4] [Xie, Z. and Bowman, J.M. "Permutation invariant polynomial neural network approach to fitting potential energy surfaces." J. Chem. Theory Comput. 6, 26-34 (2010)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/328181800_Permutation_invariant_polynomial_neural_network_approach_to_fitting_potential_energy_surfaces_IV_Coupled_diabatic_potential_energy_matrices)

The proposed eigenvector continuation ansatz uses a linear combination of global many-body wave functions, which is not size-extensive and may degrade in accuracy for larger systems or dissociation limits. "we can instead interpolate the many-body electronic wave function itself through chemical space." The ansatz $|\Psi(\mathbf{R})\rangle = \sum_a c_a |\Psi(\mathbf{R}^{(a)})\rangle$ represents a linear subspace projection. For a system composed of non-interacting subsystems A and B, the exact ground state is a product $|\Psi_A \Psi_B\rangle$. A linear combination of product states does not generally preserve this product structure unless the basis size grows exponentially with the number of subsystems. This lack of size extensivity is a known limitation of configuration interaction (CI) and reduced basis methods [3], which contrasts with the size-extensive nature of Coupled Cluster or DFT. "The method of configuration interaction ... is not size consistent." [2] Since the method is mathematically equivalent to a CI in a specific basis (the training states), it inherits the lack of size extensivity, which is a critical property for applying the method to large molecular systems or studying dissociation processes where fragments separate. References: [2] A. Szabo and N. S. Ostlund, "Modern Quantum Chemistry: Introduction to Advanced Electronic Structure Theory", Dover Publications (1996). https://store.doverpublications.com/products/9780486691862 [3] T. Helgaker, P. Jorgensen, and J. Olsen, "Molecular Electronic-Structure Theory", Wiley (2000). https://www.wiley.com/en-us/Molecular+Electronic+Structure+Theory-p-9780470011874

the introduction correctly states the problems of density functional based AIMD but fails to present a balanced view of current challenges and contemporary developments in the field. For example, AIMD of condensed phase systems utilizing correlated electronic structure methods have been developed (see e.g. Hutter et al DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-42913-7_58-1; Spura et al DOI: https://doi.org/10.1039/C4CP05192K). The introduction should reflect such recent advances.

The paper's quantitative claims about nuclear quantum effect (NQE) magnitude on proton hopping kinetics rely on MLP-based PIMD simulations that are validated against severely limited DFT PIMD data exhibiting acknowledged non-ergodic sampling behavior. "Since performing a full US simulation with 16 beads per umbrella would require a prohibitively large amount of computational resources, the MLP was used to construct an initial FES using a metadynamics simulation, so that the inverse of the obtained FES could be used as bias for the following DFT-US simulations. This allowed us to reduce the number of umbrellas to only 6, with low force constants (~25 kJ·mol⁻¹)." The DFT PIMD validation uses only 6 umbrellas compared to 39 umbrellas in the MLP simulations, representing a 6.5-fold reduction in sampling coverage. This asymmetry between the validation data and production simulations raises concerns about whether the limited DFT validation can adequately verify the MLP performance across the entire reaction coordinate, particularly in regions critical for computing kinetic rates. "Despite the good overall agreement between the MLP and DFT PIMD profiles, with variations in the order of 5 kJ·mol⁻¹ (Figure S14a), quite some difference can be seen in the steepest regions of the FES (for a collective variable ≈ ±0.7). This is most likely due to the behavior of the DFT umbrellas located in these regions, where clear non-ergodic behavior in the collective variable time series is present (Figure S14b)." The authors acknowledge that the DFT PIMD simulations exhibit "clear non-ergodic behavior" in two umbrellas near the transition state region. This is precisely the region most important for computing reaction rates. While the authors attribute the DFT-MLP discrepancy to DFT sampling issues rather than MLP inaccuracy, the inadequate DFT reference data cannot definitively validate the MLP's quantum free energy predictions, which form the basis for the claimed NQE speedup factors (65x at 273K, 16x at 373K, 7x at 473K). "Given that increasing the number of DFT umbrellas or the simulation time is currently at the edge of our available computational resources, we consider the small differences between the two FESs acceptable given the short time of the DFT simulations and the low error on the forces." This statement acknowledges the validation limitations as a computational resource constraint. However, the paper's main conclusions about NQE importance at catalytically relevant temperatures depend critically on the quantum free energy surfaces. Standard practice in MLP-driven PIMD studies, such as in Cendagorta et al. (2021) [[1]](#ref1), typically involves more rigorous validation against DFT PIMD reference data to establish quantitative reliability of NQE predictions.

The authors fail to provide the specific datasets (training structures) and input scripts necessary to reproduce the Machine Learning Potential (MLP) training and the subsequent Path Integral Molecular Dynamics (PIMD) simulations. "Energies and forces were extracted every 5 fs from the DFT US trajectories, yielding a total of ~1,200,000 training structures which were used to train an MLP with the SchNetPack package" (from Supplementary Information, Section S5.1) The training dataset of ~1.2 million structures is a critical component of the work. Without access to this specific dataset, it is impossible for other researchers to reproduce the MLP or verify its quality. "CP2K is a quantum chemistry and solid state physics software package..." (from `code/README.md` in the provided repository) The provided `code` directory contains the standard source code for the CP2K software package but lacks the specific input files (e.g., for the H-CHA system, the specific umbrella sampling parameters, or the MLP training scripts) required to replicate the study. Providing the generic software source code without the specific simulation inputs does not satisfy the requirement for reproducibility.
The reason for the high temperature leading to large barrier (as shown in Figure 3 and Figures 15-16) should be clarified properly.
It is unclear to the reader why high-temperature simulations were performed at 873 K, what is special about this temperature?

Another angle that might justify publication in Nature Comm would be the novelty of a computational approach, in this case the use of machine learning to train a potential energy surface from electronic DFT calculations, and to then use that ML surface in more extensive simulations (in this case path integral MD, or PIMD). Unfortunately, this idea is not new and it is being done quite a bit (recent work by Roberto Car on liquid water published in PNAS comes to mind). Moreover, by using a ML potential in another way, it is now possible to do highly efficient direct ab initio MD (AIMD) calculations with PIMD for the NQEs [see Li and Voth, J. Chem. Theory Comp. **18**, 599-604 (2022)]. This was not done.

The paper claims that TSGen and TSNet were "trained with the same database," but provides insufficient details about whether these re-implementations faithfully reproduce the original methods, particularly regarding TSNet's transfer learning protocol which was identified as important for its performance. "To compare the performances against the other ML models, two existing models, TSGen and TSNet, are trained with the same train dataset." The paper states that comparison models were retrained but does not specify the hyperparameters used, whether the original architectures were exactly reproduced, or the source of the implementations. Both TSGen and TSNet papers provide specific details about their architectures and training that would need to be carefully followed for a fair comparison. "Both comparison models are trained with the same database as the database used for training of the proposed model." While using the same database is necessary for fair comparison, it is not sufficient. TSNet in particular relies on transfer learning from equilibrium molecular structures (the ANI-1 dataset) to improve its performance on transition states. If this pretraining step was omitted, the comparison would be unfair to TSNet. * Quote from TSNet paper (Jackson et al.) [[2]](#citation-list): "Finally, transfer learning, a low data remedial technique, is explored to understand the viability of pretraining TSNet on widely available chemical data may provide better starting points during training, faster convergence, and lower loss values." The TSNet paper explicitly emphasizes transfer learning as a key component of their approach. The present paper does not mention whether TSNet was pretrained on equilibrium structures before fine-tuning on transition states. If transfer learning was not performed, the reported ~15% error for TSNet on bond-forming/breaking pairs (versus ~5% for the proposed method) may not represent TSNet's full capability. "TSGen exhibited slightly better performance than TSNet. However, both models showed more than two-fold greater errors (for both error metrics) compared with the proposed model." The claim of two-fold improvement is significant but requires validation through independent replication. Without access to the actual code used for training or detailed hyperparameter specifications, the community cannot verify these comparisons. References: [2] [Jackson, R., Zhang, W. & Pearson, J. TSNet: predicting transition state structures with tensor field networks and transfer learning. Chem. Sci. 12, 10022–10040 (2021).](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2021/sc/d1sc01206a)

The proposed representation and reconstruction pipeline (Validity; Data and methodology) predicts only interatomic distances and reconstructs coordinates via nonlinear optimization, which is inherently ambiguous up to reflection and therefore cannot, in general, distinguish enantiomeric or mirror-related TS geometries; this undermines claims of general 3D TS structure prediction. “The model is designed to predict the interatomic distances of TS structures … The model outputs are then converted to the predicted interatomic distances (\( d^{TS}_{ij} \)) and used to generate the atomic positions in the TS structures (\( X^* \)) by nonlinear optimization.” This describes a pipeline where the predicted object is a distance matrix, not a unique 3D geometry, and the final coordinates are obtained by a reconstruction step. Distance matrices determine structures only up to rigid motions and reflection, so the reconstruction can yield mirror images that are indistinguishable in distance space. For many applications, especially stereoselective chemistry, mirror-related TS structures are not interchangeable because they can correspond to different stereochemical outcomes or interact differently with chiral environments. The paper does not discuss or evaluate this ambiguity, yet presents the method as predicting “TS structures” in a general sense. This methodological limitation affects the validity of broad structural claims. “The pair features satisfy the translational and rotational invariances because neither atomic numbers nor distances are changed by translation or rotation.” While translational and rotational invariance are desirable, the features described here are also invariant to reflection because distances are unchanged under mirror operations. That means the model input and output spaces cannot encode chirality or handedness information required to distinguish enantiomeric configurations. The paper does not provide an additional mechanism (e.g., signed volumes or chiral constraints) to break this symmetry in reconstruction. As a result, the method can be fundamentally unable to represent certain stereochemical distinctions even before considering model error. This is not a minor detail because TS stereochemistry can be central in organic reaction outcomes. “First, we directly output Cartesians as opposed to distance matrices. … it aﬀords our model the ability to diﬀerentiate enantiomers – something not possible with only a distance matrix.” [3](#ref3) This explicitly states, in a closely related TS structure prediction paper, that distance-matrix approaches cannot distinguish enantiomers, motivating direct Cartesian (equivariant) prediction. The reviewed paper’s method is distance-matrix-based and therefore inherits this limitation unless it introduces extra orientation/chirality constraints, which it does not. This directly conflicts with broad claims of predicting general TS “structures,” because in the presence of chirality the structure is not uniquely defined by distances alone. Even if some datasets do not emphasize enantioselective reactions, the paper’s own scope statements are general enough that this limitation should be addressed. The absence of such discussion or experiments weakens the methodological completeness. “Because we use the distance matrix to generate a TS structure, the target geometry is not entirely unique, and in a few cases, this fact results in a converged TS guess that contains one or more planes of symmetry with the ground truth geometry.” [2](#ref2) This is a direct acknowledgment, from a distance-matrix TS-generation approach, that non-uniqueness can manifest as mirror-related structures and can affect downstream optimization/verification. The reviewed paper similarly reconstructs coordinates from predicted distances, but it does not report whether such mirror ambiguities occur or how often they lead to failed or incorrect TS optimizations. Without quantifying or mitigating this issue, the method’s reliability for general TS prediction remains uncertain, particularly for cases where mirror symmetry is chemically consequential. The cited evidence shows this is not a hypothetical corner case but a known issue in the immediate literature. Therefore, the reviewed paper should either constrain chirality/orientation or explicitly scope its claims.

As this is being machine learning approach, its performance/accuracy scales with the size of the training set. Although dataset of Grambow et al. has vast number of reactions, real applications always involve building up their own reaction data and this is even more stipulated by the failure case given in this manuscript. In this work training-validation-test set as splited into 8-1-1 ratio, but would be interesting to present performance improvement with respect to number of training set (learning curve). Then readers might be able to expect approximated accuracy when trained with this model.
Regarding saddle-point optimization, author should clarify which specific algorithm has been used in Qchem and which convergence criteria has been imposed in this work. (e.g. Minimum force)

I was particularly impressed with the authors ensemble approach, which endows the model with the ability to find multiple TS structures between a particular pair of RC and PC. This is a particularly clever solution to the problem of PES complexity.

I especially like the modeling choice to predict a multiplier to the ratio of the interpolated distance matrix and the TS distance matrix. Did you test predicting the distance value directly? I’m curious to know if this makes a substantial difference (not a necessary experiment).

To train the TS model, Choi uses random splits on the Grambow dataset. However, several recent publications have noted the issues with using random splits on this dataset (see ref. 1 and 2). While the dataset contains ~12k unique reactions, it only contains ~1-2k unique reactants. So, when using a random split, some of the reactants may be duplicated in the test set. For TS geometry prediction, this is not a severe issue, but I suggest reproducing Fig. 2 using reactant-based scaffold splits (see ref. 2 for additional details). There’s no need to redo the QM optimization section. [1].Heid, E., & Green, W. H. (2021). Machine learning of reaction properties via learned representations of the condensed graph of reaction. Journal of chemical information and modeling. [2].Spiekermann, Kevin A., Lagnajit Pattanaik, and William H. Green. \"Fast Predictions of Reaction Barrier Heights: Toward Coupled-Cluster Accuracy.\" The Journal of Physical Chemistry A 126.25 (2022): 3976-3986.

The comparison between NequIP (133 training structures) and DeepMD (133,500 training structures) in Table III does not constitute a valid data efficiency comparison because it compares different architectures at vastly different operating points rather than showing learning curves for both methods. "A DeepMD NN-IP model was previously trained [15] for water and ice using a joint training set containing 133,500 reference calculations of these four systems. To assess data efficiency of the NequIP architecture, we similarly train a model jointly on all four parts of the data set, but using only 133 structures for training, i.e. 1000x fewer data." While this comparison shows that NequIP achieves better force RMSE with 133 structures than DeepMD does with 133,500 structures, it does not actually demonstrate "data efficiency" in the proper sense. Data efficiency would require showing that NequIP consistently achieves lower errors than DeepMD at the same training set size, or equivalently, that NequIP reaches a target accuracy with fewer data points. The comparison as presented could simply mean that NequIP is a fundamentally better architecture regardless of data size, rather than being more "data-efficient." "TABLE III: Root mean square error (RMSE) of force components on liquid water and the three ices in units of [meV/Å]. Note that the NequIP model was trained on < 0.1% of the training data of DeepMD." A proper data efficiency study would train both NequIP and DeepMD on identical training set sizes (e.g., 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 10000 structures) and plot learning curves showing error as a function of training data size. This is precisely what the paper does for the l=0 vs l=0/l=1 comparison in Figure 7, but fails to do for the DeepMD comparison. Without this, the 1000x claim is scientifically imprecise because we cannot determine where the crossover point is or whether DeepMD might actually be more data-efficient at certain data regimes. "The algorithm learns as new data become available. Empirical results have shown that this exponent typically remains fixed across different learning algorithms for a given data set, and different methods only shift the learning curve, leaving the log-log slope unaffected" [[1]](#citation-list) The published Nature Communications version acknowledges that different methods often have similar scaling exponents, meaning they learn at similar rates as data increases. The preprint's comparison at a single point (133 vs 133,500) cannot capture this scaling behavior, which is essential for characterizing data efficiency. References: [1] Batzner, S. et al. "E(3)-equivariant graph neural networks for data-efficient and accurate interatomic potentials." [Nature Communications 13, 2453 (2022)](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-29939-5)

This paper’s methodological conclusion that the performance/data-efficiency gains are directly caused by the SE(3)-equivariant convolution architecture is not convincingly established, because the presented ablation is limited in scope and does not robustly isolate equivariance from other confounded factors (e.g., representation capacity, split variance, and evaluation difficulty). "We directly verify that the performance gains are connected with the unique SE(3)-equivariant convolution architecture of the new NequIP model." This is a causal attribution claim, not merely a descriptive performance report, and therefore requires especially careful experimental controls. In ML architecture studies, isolating a causal factor typically involves multiple controlled ablations, matched capacity, and repeated runs across splits/seeds to quantify variance. The paper later provides a specific ablation, but it is limited to a single test system and a single form of “turning off” vector interactions. Without stronger controls, the statement “directly verify” overstates what is actually demonstrated. Because the paper’s broader significance rests on equivariance being the key mechanism, this unsupported causal leap materially weakens the conclusions. "In order to gain insight into the reasons behind increased accuracy and data efficiency, we perform a series of experiments with the goal of isolating the effect of using equivariant convolutions of geometric tensors compared to invariant convolutions over scalars. ... Second, when all interactions involving \( l = 1 \) are turned off, this turns the network into a conventional invariant GNN-IP, involving only invariant convolutions over scalar features in a SchNet-style fashion." Turning off all \(l=1\) interactions changes not only equivariance properties but also the representational form and information flow (e.g., removing vector channels and associated tensor products). Even if hyperparameters are “identical,” the effective model class and expressivity can change substantially, so attributing all differences to equivariance alone is not cleanly justified. Additionally, labeling the ablated model as “SchNet-style” does not guarantee equivalence to SchNet’s exact architecture, optimization, and inductive biases, which makes interpretation ambiguous. Since the paper claims to have “isolated” the effect, it should demonstrate that other factors (capacity, parameter count, training stability, and evaluation variance) are controlled or measured. Without that, the ablation supports “equivariant performs better than this particular non-equivariant variant” but not the stronger causal claim. "Finally, we introduce a more challenging benchmark with chemical and structural variations that suggests the path for further work." ([5](#citation-list)) This highlights that, even within closely related molecular ML potential literature, the community recognizes the need for benchmarks that stress generalization beyond standard MD trajectory interpolation. The reviewed paper’s equivariance ablation is performed on a single bulk-water dataset with random sampling from a finite set of configurations, which may not reflect these harder generalization regimes. If the benchmark is not challenging enough (e.g., due to “twin” configurations or near-interpolation), architectural differences can be amplified or suppressed in ways that do not transfer to harder settings. Therefore, using only such an evaluation to claim a mechanistic explanation for broad data-efficiency gains is insufficient. The cited sentence supports the expectation that stronger, more varied benchmarks are needed before making sweeping causal conclusions about architectural choices. "Averaging an error metric over all *k* folds yields a measure that is less affected by random noise induced by the exact choice of train-/test-set splitting." ([2](#citation-list)) The reviewed paper’s architectural attribution relies on experiments that use randomly sampled training sets from correlated MD data, but it does not report repeated trials over multiple splits/seeds for the ablation. Without quantifying split-induced variance, it is difficult to know whether the observed gap is stable or partly an artifact of a favorable/unfavorable sampling. The cited sentence articulates why multi-split evaluation (e.g., k-fold or repeated splits) is important to reduce sensitivity to one particular split. Since the paper’s claim is about the *reason* behind data efficiency, it is not enough to show a single curve; it must show robustness across splits. This gap weakens the strength of the mechanistic conclusion.

In the comparison of MD17 performance shown in table I, indeed the NequIP shows indeed an amazing performance. Nevertheless, in the comparison to other approached including NequIP(I=0)=SchNet, the discussion reads somehow flat (what is being told is just reading table I). Here, a more insightful discussion should be added, such as elaborating on the core differences between the compared methods and explain why a neural net of this nature can outperform even kernel methods.

In the case of Li4P2O7, I cannot give a proper evaluation of the application if the RMSE are missing but more importantly, because of the missing energy errors. The energetics in diffusion is crucial, and because of this, I’m not sure how to judge the reported diffusivity values. Furthermore, this application needs a convergence analysis (diffusivity vs length of the simulations) since 50 ps is an extremely short time for such a macroscopic variable. Hence, in order to discard any possible artefacts of the NequIP, such analysis should be provided.

Page 9, line 446: The authors wrote \"ability to achieve high accuracy on a comparatively small data set opens the door to training models on expensive high-order ab-initio quantum chemical methods.\" This seems to be a bit overstated, as previous MLPs that trained on CCSD(T) and QMC already exist.

The genotoxicity assessment was performed only for the 11.7T group using before/after intra-subject comparisons without a 0T control group, which is methodologically inconsistent with how other safety measures were evaluated and may be insufficient given that prior literature has demonstrated genotoxic effects from repeated MRI exposure. "Genotoxicity was also assessed but only for the 11.7T group with blood samples drawn before and after the 90 min exposure to perform intra-subject comparisons." While the behavioral, vestibular, and physiological tests included both 11.7T and 0T control groups for between-group comparisons, the genotoxicity assessment inexplicably omits the 0T control group. This creates an asymmetry in the study design where arguably the most concerning potential safety outcome (genetic damage) receives less rigorous evaluation than other measures. An intra-subject pre/post design without a control group cannot account for time-dependent changes or procedural effects unrelated to magnetic field exposure. "No statistically significant differences in micronuclei count for genotoxicity for each subject before versus after exposure at 11.7T were found either." The absence of statistical significance in micronuclei counts from a single 90-minute exposure in 20 subjects has limited interpretability. Reference [15] cited in the paper (Herate et al., 2022 [[4]](#citation-4)) specifically examined effects of "repeated brain MRI" at 3T. The current study's single-exposure design may be insufficient to detect cumulative genotoxic effects that could emerge with the repeated exposures typical of clinical or research MRI use. The authors cite this reference but do not adequately address why single-exposure testing is sufficient given the documented effects from repeated exposures. "Having shown that the repetitive exposure to 3T in MRI [15] induces mainly terminal deletions, the micronuclei protocol was performed with cytochalasin B addition at 68h after lymphocyte growth stimulation" The authors acknowledge that their reference [15] demonstrated genotoxic effects from repeated MRI exposure. However, the study design tests only single exposure, which does not address whether repeated 11.7T exposures would produce similar or enhanced effects. Given that chronic or repeated exposure has been identified as the relevant exposure scenario for genotoxicity concerns, the single-exposure design represents a significant gap in the safety assessment. References: [4] Herate, C., Brochard, P., De Vathaire, F., et al. "The effects of repeated brain MRI on chromosomal damage." European Radiology Experimental 6, 12 (2022). https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s41747-022-00264-2

The paper’s causal interpretation that observed bioeffects outcomes reflect “no effect of field exposure” (rather than psychological or procedural confounds) is weakened by the non-randomized sequential cohort design and limited reporting on blinding effectiveness and protocol stability. "Those tests were performed on the 20 subjects scanned at 11.7T and on another cohort of 20 subjects with the magnetic field switched off, to disentangle any effect from a possible psychological bias (nocebo effect)." The manuscript explicitly frames the 0T cohort as enabling causal disentanglement of field effects from psychological bias, which is a strong claim that typically requires careful control of allocation, blinding, and protocol stability. However, the paper does not report objective checks of blinding success (e.g., whether subjects correctly guessed condition, or whether staff were blinded) beyond stating that participants were not informed. It also does not report whether any protocol elements changed over time (e.g., operator practice, task instructions, sequence tweaks) that could differentially affect the first and second cohort. Because “nocebo disentanglement” is a central support for the safety conclusion, these missing design/reporting elements weaken the internal validity of the comparison. In the absence of such controls, “no significant differences” is consistent with multiple alternative explanations beyond “no field effect.” "To minimize the number of field ramps for the magnet, the study first included the 0T group, followed by the 11.7T one." This sentence makes clear that group assignment was not randomized and that the cohorts were run in a fixed order, which introduces temporal confounding (learning effects in operators, protocol drift, seasonal/organizational changes, or differing recruitment). In biomedical research, non-randomized sequential allocation can bias group comparisons even when baseline demographics appear similar, particularly for behavioral and subjective endpoints. The manuscript does not provide mitigation strategies for this design choice (e.g., alternating conditions, pre-registered analysis, or sensitivity analyses), nor does it present evidence that measurement conditions were stable across the study period. Because the core inference is based on between-group differences (11.7T vs 0T), the fixed-order design reduces the credibility of the causal claim. This is a substantial internal-validity limitation rather than a minor procedural detail. "Vertigo was apparently more frequent and severe at 7T, but unpleasantness by moving in and out of the scanners was noted even at 0T, although the severity was higher at 7T. Moreover, five subjects noticed mild magnetic field at 0T." [[1]](#cite-1) This literature quote demonstrates two problems that directly apply to the reviewed paper’s nocebo-control logic: (i) subjective discomfort can occur even in 0T mock conditions, and (ii) participants may infer (correctly or incorrectly) field presence, compromising blinding. The reviewed paper tries to mimic gradient noise and keep subjects uninformed, but it does not report whether subjects detected the condition or whether expectancy differed between cohorts. The literature indicates that such factors can materially influence subjective and behavioral outcomes, meaning that simple “0T + same instructions” may be insufficient to fully isolate nocebo effects. This matters because the paper’s conclusion hinges on the idea that psychological bias is neutralized by design. Without explicit checks, the disentanglement claim is not well supported. "Proper randomisation reduces selection bias at trial entry and is the crucial component of high quality RCTs." [[5]](#cite-5) While the reviewed study is not a therapeutic RCT, it makes a causal claim that depends on between-group comparability, which is exactly the context where randomization is the standard bias-reduction tool. The paper’s stated reason for fixed ordering (minimizing field ramps) is operationally understandable, but it does not address the resulting risk of bias in interpreting group differences as field effects. The CONSORT elaboration emphasizes that without randomization, selection and confounding biases can prejudice comparisons, especially when outcomes include subjective experiences and cognitive task performance. The reviewed manuscript’s reporting does not include compensatory design features (e.g., cross-over design, counterbalancing, or randomized assignment) that would restore the strength of the causal inference. Therefore, the claimed separation of nocebo and field effects is methodologically weaker than presented. References: [1] Okada T, Akasaka T, Thuy DHD, Isa T. *Safety for Human MR Scanners at 7T*. Magnetic Resonance in Medical Sciences (2022). https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9618930/ [5] Moher D, Hopewell S, Schulz KF, et al. *CONSORT 2010 Explanation and Elaboration: updated guidelines for reporting parallel group randomised trials* (PDF). BMJ (2010). https://www.bmj.com/content/bmj/340/bmj.c869.full.pdf

In order to maximize the impact of this scientific report, it is important to provide open access to the initial data presented in figure 2. This will pique the curiosity of the community and greatly increase the number of citations for this study. Although the authors have stated that \"All data supporting the findings of this study are available within the paper and its Extended Method,\" I was unable to locate this information in the materials provided for review. Sharing data is now more accessible than ever through platforms like neurovault.org, which I highly recommend to the authors.

While I understand that the authors have 3T and 7T scanners available at their facility. I would argue that the relevant reference point would be the human imaging results obtained at the field strength of 10.5T. A more detailed comparison with published findings at this field strength (closest to the one used here) would be highly relevant.
The project name Iseult is used throughout the manuscript, but it is unclear what the specific meaning is?

The paper reports the first measurement of the 2S hyperfine splitting (HFS) in muonium (559.6(72) MHz) and compares it to a "theoretical value" of 557.9 MHz with uncertainty "<0.1 MHz." However, this theoretical value is not derived from rigorous bound-state QED calculations but rather from a simple 1/n³ scaling of the measured 1S HFS, which neglects state-specific QED corrections that differ between n=1 and n=2 levels. "The value was estimated by taking the most precise 1S HFS measurement in muonium [12] and dividing it by a factor of 2³. The uncertainty was estimated by calculating the QED D₂₁ difference [35]." This footnote in Table I reveals that the theoretical 2S HFS value is derived by scaling the experimental 1S HFS measurement of 4463.302765(53) MHz ([Liu et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 82, 711 (1999)][2]) by 1/8, yielding ~557.9 MHz. While the 1/n³ scaling is the leading-order behavior for hydrogen-like atoms, it neglects state-specific QED corrections. The D₂₁ correction mentioned represents only one component of the difference between 1S and 2S hyperfine structure corrections. Modern QED calculations of hyperfine structure include terms such as higher-order binding corrections, radiative-recoil corrections, and state-dependent nuclear structure effects. For a first measurement claiming to determine "for the first time the 2S hyperfine splitting in Muonium," a comparison to a rigorous QED calculation would strengthen the scientific significance of the result. "Theoretical value HFS (*): 557.9, <0.1" The presentation of this scaled value as a "Theoretical value" in Table I alongside the rigorous theoretical Lamb shift value of 1047.498(0.001) MHz (from Janka, Ohayon, and Crivelli [23]) creates a misleading impression of equivalent theoretical rigor. The Lamb shift theoretical value cited is based on comprehensive QED calculations, while the 2S HFS "theoretical value" is an experimental value divided by eight with an uncertainty estimate from a specific correction term. The paper would benefit from either citing proper QED calculations for the 2S HFS or clearly distinguishing the nature of these two theoretical comparisons. * Quote (from [Karshenboim, Physics Reports 422, 1 (2005)][3]): "The hyperfine interval in muonium has attracted a lot of attention because of the possibility of high-precision measurements... The theoretical uncertainty is mainly due to unknown higher-order contributions." This review article on precision physics of simple atoms emphasizes that hyperfine structure calculations require careful treatment of multiple QED corrections. The scaling approach used in the paper under review, while providing a reasonable estimate, does not capture the full complexity of state-dependent QED effects that affect the 2S HFS differently from the 1S HFS. References: [2] W. Liu et al., "High precision measurements of the ground state hyperfine structure interval of muonium and of the muon magnetic moment," [Phys. Rev. Lett. 82, 711 (1999)](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.82.711) [3] S. G. Karshenboim, "Precision physics of simple atoms: QED tests, nuclear structure and fundamental constants," [Physics Reports 422, 1 (2005)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0370157305003625)

The manuscript reports extracted values for the Muonium Lamb shift and the \(2S\) hyperfine splitting, but it omits essential derivational and data-reporting details (including the per-frequency data and the combination procedure with prior measurements), limiting independent verification and reproducibility under **Data and methodology**, and weakening confidence in the **Conclusions** about the first determination of the \(2S\) HFS. "From this measurement we extract the \( 2S_{1/2}, F=0 \rightarrow 2P_{1/2}, F=1 \) resonance frequency to be 580.6(68) MHz and determine the M LS to be 1045.5(68) MHz." This reports a derived LS value but does not show, within the preprint, the explicit relation used to convert a hyperfine-resolved transition frequency into the centroid Lamb shift. In the \(I=J=1/2\) case, this conversion requires incorporating hyperfine shifts for the specific \(F\) levels and therefore depends on adopted \(2S\) and \(2P\) hyperfine splittings. The preprint does not clearly state which hyperfine splitting values (measured vs. theoretical) were used for the conversion, nor does it provide the intermediate calculation. While the numerical values are plausible, the lack of explicit derivation prevents an independent reader from checking whether the LS definition, sign conventions, and inputs match common practice. This is particularly important because the manuscript’s narrative emphasizes isolating a specific hyperfine transition to reduce systematics, so the mapping from that transition to the LS should be transparent and verifiable. Without that transparency, the LS result is not fully reproducible from the manuscript. "Using our previous results of the \( 2S_{1/2}, F=1 \rightarrow 2P_{1/2}, F=1 \) resonance frequency [20], we extract for the first time the 2S hyperfine splitting in Muonium to be 559.6(72) MHz." This claim depends on combining the present measurement with a prior measurement, but the preprint does not describe the statistical procedure used for the combination (e.g., whether a joint fit was performed, how correlations are treated, and what theoretical inputs were fixed). Because both measurements are performed by the same collaboration in related apparatus configurations, correlated systematics are plausible and can materially affect the uncertainty on a derived difference like the 2S HFS. The manuscript also does not provide the underlying data points for the earlier resonance scan, so the “first time” HFS determination cannot be independently reconstructed from the preprint alone. In precision spectroscopy, derived splittings are typically accompanied by the explicit formula used and a clear accounting of correlated uncertainties. The absence of such detail makes the reported 559.6(72) MHz difficult to independently validate and therefore weakens the strength of the conclusion. "* The value was estimated by taking the most precise 1S HFS measurement in muonium [12] and dividing it by a factor of \(2^3\). The uncertainty was estimated by calculating the QED \(D_{21}\) difference [35]." This footnote shows that the paper’s “theoretical value HFS (*)” is not a standalone ab initio prediction for the 2S HFS, but an estimate derived from experimental 1S HFS plus a theory-motivated correction. Presenting this as a “Theoretical value” in Table I can overstate how independently the measured 2S HFS is being validated against theory. For a first measurement claim, a clear separation between (i) experimentally inferred quantities and (ii) independent theoretical benchmarks is important to interpret “agreement with theory.” If the benchmark partially inherits the experimental 1S HFS input, the comparison is less stringent than it appears. This matters for conclusions about consistency with bound-state QED, especially if future work aims at kHz-level tests where such distinctions become critical. As written, the manuscript’s presentation risks giving a stronger impression of theory validation than is warranted by an estimated reference value. "To estimate the inﬂuence of the simulation input pa-rameters on the resonance position, we scan them around their uncertainty and repeat the analysis." ([2](#ref2)) This excerpt from the authors’ prior muonium LS work exemplifies a standard reproducibility and robustness practice in simulation-driven spectroscopy: explicitly varying key inputs and repeating the full analysis to quantify induced line-center shifts. In the reviewed manuscript, multiple simulation-derived elements (power correction factors, field maps, and 3S contamination modeling) can influence the fitted offset, but the preprint does not report a comparable input-sensitivity study for the current measurement. The contrast matters because the present result already shows large fit sensitivity to inclusion of extra components, making input-parameter robustness especially important. Without providing either the data or a documented sensitivity study, independent readers cannot judge whether the stated uncertainties appropriately cover simulation/model uncertainty. Thus, the methodology disclosure in the reviewed manuscript falls short of what the field commonly reports for simulation-dominated inferences. This directly limits reproducibility and confidence in the derived LS and 2S HFS. "Here, a direct measurement of the *n* = 2 Lamb shift of atomic hydrogen is presented." ([5](#ref5)) This statement highlights why “direct” Lamb-shift determinations are of broad interest: they are intended to rely on well-defined spectroscopic observables that can be independently checked and reanalyzed. For such measurements, the community expectation is that the mapping from measured transition frequencies to the reported Lamb shift (including hyperfine and other corrections) is fully specified, enabling independent reproduction from the reported inputs. The reviewed manuscript’s LS and 2S HFS values are presented without enough explicit derivational detail and without tabulation of the underlying frequency-point data beyond a single off-resonance point. As a result, the work is harder to independently verify than is typical for high-impact spectroscopy results built around “direct” determinations. This does not negate the measurement, but it weakens the reproducibility and interpretability of the derived quantities. In turn, that limits how strongly the paper can support its conclusions about first determinations and agreement with theory.

The precision of the reported measurement is significantly lower than the collaboration's previous work, undermining the claim that this transition is the "best candidate" for future measurements without further demonstration of improved systematics. "From this measurement we extract the 2S_{1/2}, F=0 -> 2P_{1/2}, F=1 resonance frequency to be 580.6(68) MHz" The reported uncertainty is 6.8 MHz. "Our result ... is limited by the statistical uncertainty." The authors admit the measurement is statistically limited. "Combining this result with our previous measurement [20]..." The previous measurement by the same collaboration (Ref [20]) achieved an uncertainty of 2.5 MHz. The current measurement is nearly factor of 3 less precise. While measuring a new transition is valuable, the paper argues that this transition is superior ("best candidate") because it is "isolated". However, the current result does not demonstrate this advantage in practice, as it is dominated by statistics and potentially 3S line-pulling, yielding a result that is less precise than existing data.

Authors write in Abstract: „...which might provide an explanation of the g-2 muon anomaly or Lorentz and CPT violation...”. While muon g-2 is a well established anomaly, it is not the case of Lorentz and CPT violation. I would not put them on the same footing.

[Introduction section] The reviewer finds the first three paragraphs somewhat essayistic, not to say unnecessary, but not deeply relevant enough to be the basis for later discussion. The reviewer suggests compressing the descriptions.
[Experiment section] The phrase “an electrical field of the order of 250 Vcm-1” in the fourth paragraph seems a bit odd (too specific to be “an order”). Why not simply use about 250 Vcm-1 here?
[Experiment section] Why is the correction factor C(f) determined by the effectively measured power even though microwave power is independent of frequency?

[Results section] How accurate is the assumption that Lya and Lyb have similar detection efficiency? Is it possible to discuss the degree of uncertainty, even if it is difficult to evaluate precisely?
To my taste, there are too many abbreviations in general and per a sentence.

While the paper claims to observe a state "compatible with being a Tcc+ tetraquark state," the analysis does not provide sufficient evidence to distinguish between a compact tetraquark configuration and a loosely bound D\*D hadronic molecular state. The proximity of the observed mass to the D\*+D0 threshold (δm ≈ −273 keV/c²) is equally consistent with either interpretation, yet this important theoretical question is not adequately addressed. "In this Letter, the observation of a narrow state in the D0D0π+ mass spectrum near the D\*+D0 mass threshold compatible with being a Tcc+ tetraquark state is reported." The identification as a "tetraquark" is presented cautiously but without discussion of alternative interpretations. Given that "Due to the closeness of their masses to known particle-pair thresholds, many of these states are likely to be hadronic molecules" as stated in the introduction, the assignment requires more careful consideration. The extreme proximity to threshold (< 1 MeV below) is actually more characteristic of molecular states in many theoretical frameworks. "The near-threshold mass together with a strikingly narrow width reveals the resonance nature of the state." While this statement establishes that a resonance is observed, it does not address whether the state is a compact tetraquark or a molecular configuration. The theoretical literature cited in the paper (Refs. [41-46]) discusses both possibilities for QQq̄q̄ systems, and distinguishing between them requires additional observables such as production cross-sections, decay patterns, or size-related measurements. * Quote from Ref. [[2]](#citation-list): "The proximity of this maximum to the intersection of the two M(D\*+) dashed straight lines is a cautionary signal of a possible kinematic enhancement." The follow-up theoretical work by Karliner and Rosner explicitly notes that the observed signal's proximity to the kinematic crossing point raises questions about its interpretation, suggesting the need for caution in assigning a purely resonant tetraquark interpretation without additional evidence. References: [2] M. Karliner and J. L. Rosner, "The doubly charmed strange tetraquark," arXiv:2110.12054 (2022). [https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.12054](https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.12054)

[Overall significance] This manuscript describes an observation of a new type hadron containing four quarks, namely cc\bar{u}\bar{d}, which is beyond the conventional quark model telling us only baryons and mesons. The results are original and has great impact to the particle physics field usually studying sub-atom particles. It helps complete the exotic hadron picture, which is popular and of interest in a wide community over the last two decades. Within my knowledge, I think the authors credited previous work properly, except for the tetraquark candidate observed by Belle in 2013 (Phys. Rev. Lett. 110, 252002), which should be added in the reference as well.

[Strength of the claims] P2, 3rd para, L6: In the fit to the signal, the author use a P-wave resonance, and treat other options as systematic. However, in my opinion, the D^0pi^+ system is very likely to be a virtue D*+ (which is a vector), as indicated also by D^0pi^+ events in the higher mass region. Thus, it seems S-wave resonance is the most natural parameterization method, still within the J^P=1^+ assumption.
[Strength of the claims] P6, Selection, L8: The mass window of D0 is 130 MeV, what's the D0 mass resolution here? In page 2, the authors mention the resolution is 400 keV.
[Strength of the claims] P8, item 2, last line: is it possible to parameterize the background component as the sum of phase space (2-body or 3-body) and polynomial?
[Strength of the claims] Is a definition of pseudo rapidity required.

The paper’s physical interpretation asserts that CP symmetry should hold in the \(\Lambda_b^0 \to R(p\pi^-)R(K^-\pi^+)\) topology because the tree amplitude does not contribute, yet the measured asymmetry in that region is non-negligible; the manuscript does not reconcile this tension (e.g., via look-elsewhere corrected significance or demonstrating consistency with residual systematics), weakening the reliability of the interpretation. "The \( \Lambda_b^0 \to R(p\pi^-)R(K^-\pi^+) \) decay involves a \( b \to d\bar{d}s \) transition, where the \( d \) quark forms the \( R(p\pi^-) \) baryon together with \( ud \) quarks from the initial \( \Lambda_b^0 \) baryon and the remaining \( \bar{d}s \) quarks forms the \( R(K^-\pi^+) \) meson. In this case, the tree amplitude does not contribute, and thus \( CP \) symmetry is expected to hold." This is a strong, testable theoretical statement embedded in the manuscript’s interpretation section. When such a topology is used as a quasi-null expectation, a measured nonzero asymmetry in that region becomes an important internal consistency check of the analysis. If the measured asymmetry is compatible with zero after accounting for multiple testing and systematic uncertainties, the paper should explicitly show that consistency to avoid confusion. Conversely, if the asymmetry remains notable, the statement CP symmetry is expected to hold may be too simplified for the selected mass window (which can still contain interfering contributions or residual tree pollution). The manuscript does not provide the quantitative reconciliation needed to support its interpretive narrative. "\( \Lambda_b^0 \to R(p\pi^-)R(K^-\pi^+) \)" and "(2.7 0.8 0.1)%" Numerically, this corresponds to a roughly 3 deviation from zero if one uses the quoted uncertainties in the same way the manuscript uses them elsewhere (i.e., dividing by the total uncertainty). Even if the authors later argue that this is not significant after accounting for the look-elsewhere effect across multiple regions, the paper does not report the corrected p-value or corrected significance for this specific region. Because the interpretation claims CP should hold here, leaving this apparent deviation unquantified is problematic: it can either indicate residual experimental bias in that region or reflect inadequacy of the simplified topology-level argument. Either way, the absence of a clear reconciliation weakens confidence in the data interpretation. Reporting the look-elsewhere corrected significance (and/or a dedicated systematic cross-check) for this null-expected region would materially improve the robustness of the conclusions. "When searching for a new resonance somewhere in a possible mass range, the significance of observing a local excess of events must take into account the probability of observing such an excess anywhere in the range. This is the so called \"look elsewhere effect\"." ([[2]](#ref2)) While Gross & Vitells discuss look-elsewhere effects for resonance searches, the underlying principle applies generally: when multiple correlated regions or selections are examined, local significances can be inflated relative to global significance. The manuscript acknowledges a look-elsewhere correction for the local \(\mathcal{A}_{CP}\) measurements, but it does not provide the corrected significance values for each region, including the topology where CP is expected to hold. Given the tension between expectation and the quoted central value, readers need the globally adjusted significance (or an explicit statement that the result becomes fully compatible with zero) to interpret this check correctly. Without this, the narrative risks appearing internally inconsistent: CP should hold is stated, yet a seemingly notable asymmetry is reported in the corresponding region. The external reference supports why the paper should explicitly quantify how multiple testing affects this apparent deviation. References: [2] [E. Gross, O. Vitells, Trial factors for the look elsewhere effect in high energy physics (arXiv:1005.1891)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1005.1891)
Line 11: perhaps “dominance of matter” would be more clear and accurate than “persistence of matter”;

I feel it is not clearly spelled out what is actually measured: It is said that the CP asymmetry in the decay Lambda_b to p K- pi+ pi- is studied. However, you do not measure the direct decay as intermediate resonant contributions are included (and probably are even the dominant contribution to the CP asymmetry). On the other hand charmed resonances are vetoed (this is the control channel). What you actually measure becomes clear only after looking at the Feyman diagrams in Fig. 1.\n\nSo, I would write in the abstract and in the introduction that you study the CP asymmetry in Lambda_b decays to p K- pi+ pi- corresponding to b -> u or b -> s quark level transitions.
Please spell out which charm resonances you veto: Is it only Lambda_c -> p K- pi+ and D0 -> K- pi+ or are there also other modes (like D+ -> K- pi+ pi-).

For the LiH crystal, the paper uses a HF-based finite-size correction from a 2×2×2 to extrapolate thermodynamic properties, but performs the larger 3×3×3 calculation at only a single geometry (equilibrium lattice constant) and at the Γ-point only, without twist averaging. Standard practice in QMC studies of solids typically employs twist-averaged boundary conditions (TABC) for 3D systems to reduce finite-size errors, as the authors themselves use for graphene. "For further validation, we have also computed directly the 3 × 3 × 3 supercell of LiH at its equilibrium length 4.061Å, which contains 108 electrons." The 3×3×3 calculation is performed at only a single lattice constant, making it impossible to assess how the method performs across the equation of state for this larger system. The comparison "also very close to the experimental value −4.759 eV" relies heavily on the accuracy of the HF-based finite-size correction rather than demonstrating convergence with system size through twist averaging as done for graphene. "The calculations are performed on a 2 × 2 × 2 supercell of graphene using twist average boundary condition (TABC) [25] in conjunction with structure factor S(k) correction [26] (see Supplementary Fig. 3) to reduce the finite-size error." The paper applies TABC for graphene but not for LiH, despite LiH being a 3D system where finite-size errors are typically more severe. A recent transferable neural wavefunction study [[2]](#citation-2) demonstrated that for LiH specifically, joint optimization across multiple twists and system sizes significantly reduces computational cost while improving accuracy. The absence of TABC for LiH in this work represents an inconsistency in methodology across systems. "Using our neural network, we first simulate the equation of state of LiH on a 2 × 2 × 2 supercell, as shown in Fig. 2f. In addition, we employ a standard finite-size correction based on Hartree-Fock calculations of a large supercell" The finite-size correction methodology relies on HF calculations rather than using neural network calculations at multiple supercell sizes to extrapolate to the thermodynamic limit. While this is computationally pragmatic, it introduces uncertainty about how much of the reported cohesive energy accuracy is attributable to the neural network versus the HF correction. Standard QMC practice often involves calculating multiple system sizes to directly quantify and extrapolate finite-size effects [[3]](#citation-3). References: [2] Gerard, L., Scherbela, M., Sutterud, H., Foulkes, W. M. C. & Grohs, P. Transferable neural wavefunctions for solids. [Nat. Comput. Sci. 5, 1147–1157 (2025)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43588-025-00872-z). [3] Chiesa, S., Ceperley, D. M., Martin, R. M. & Holzmann, M. Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions. [Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404 (2006)](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404).

The manuscript reports many high-precision quantitative outcomes (e.g., “within 0.1 eV/atom,” “error of less than 1%”) without clearly defining statistical uncertainties, their estimators, or how uncertainties propagate through derived metrics like cohesive energies and “correlation error,” which weakens the reliability of the conclusions. “Supplementary Figure 2 | \(2 \times 2\) Graphene training curve. For clarity, at each iteration number, we show the median energy per primitive cell over the last 10% of iteration.” Reporting a “median … over the last 10%” is a visualization choice that does not, by itself, provide a statistically interpretable uncertainty estimate for the final energies. Medians suppress outliers but do not quantify sampling variance, autocorrelation effects, or residual optimization drift, all of which are central in VMC-style estimations.
